{{Shrine | name = Nagahama Shrine (長浜神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 2.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | location = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)}} 1-8-7 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} | Shrine_rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | consecration_year = Tradition dates to {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}} 13 (1406 CE) | Main_building = | Other_name = Nagahama-sama[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2016/06/21/135546234 「雨の長浜さま」あれこれ【おおいた祭時記】] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, June 21, 2016 | Events = Annual festival: July 5–7 | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Nagahama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 1.jpg|thumb|right|Torii gate]] [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 3.jpg|thumb|right|Hina ema (wooden plaques)]] '''Nagahama Shrine''' (長浜神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}. The shrine's primary deity is {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}}[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2015/12/28/132221197 新年の舞「めおと」で 雌の獅子頭奉納 大分市の長浜神社] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, December 28, 2015, and it also enshrines {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}}[http://www.niigata-u.com/files/oita/nagahama.html Nagahama Shrine (Nagahama Town, Ōita City)] Ōita Shrine Exploration. Under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度}}, the shrine was classified as a gōsha. Nagahama Shrine is particularly revered by women for its association with the healing of gynecological ailments, as it enshrines deities regarded as patrons of medicine, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}[http://www.i-oita.net/spot/history_culture/oita/9182.html Nagahama Shrine] iNavi Ōita (Ōita Broadcasting System). Devotees traditionally offer wooden plaques (ema) depicting male and female hina dolls when making prayersKatsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 12: Around Nagahama" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita012a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, November 2, 2007. == History == According to shrine tradition, Nagahama Shrine was founded in {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}} 13 (1406). A local resident of Nagahama had a divine dream, and the following morning discovered a small wooden shrine box washed ashore, containing a miniature altar inscribed "Yoshu Nagahama Daimyōjin" on the inner side of its door and a sacred mirror inside. Upon reporting this discovery to {{ill|Ōtomo Chikayo|ja|大友親世}}, the {{ill|Governor of Bungo Province|ja|豊後国守護}}, Chikayo donated shrine land and funds for construction. The shrine was established and named "Nagahama Daimyōjin"Katsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 1: Around Shōwa Avenue" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, August 17, 2007. The shrine was damaged by the tsunami caused by the {{ill|Keichō Bungo earthquake|ja|慶長豊後地震}} on {{ill|July 12 (lunar calendar)|ja|7月12日 (旧暦)}} in {{ill|Bunroku|ja|文禄}} 5 (1596 CE). It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} 9 (1681 CE). In {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正}} 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from {{ill|July 5|ja|7月5日}} to {{ill|July 7|ja|7月7日}}, is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿}} processions, {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}, and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the {{ill|Fuchū domain (Bungo)|ja|府内藩}}, {{ill|Matsudaira Chikayoshi|ja|松平近説}}, was halted in 1922 but resumed in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of {{ill|Ligustrum japonicum|ja|ネズミモチ|lt=Privet}} adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōita Station|ja|大分駅}}, {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|九州旅客鉄道}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ōita]] [[Category:History of Ōita]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[ja:%E9%95%B7%E6%B5%9C%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E5%A4%A7%E5%88%86%E5%B8%82)]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[ja:&action=edit]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:images]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:長浜神社]]

The tradition dates to the {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōita Station|ja|大分駅}}, {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|九州旅客鉄道}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ōita]] [[Category:History of Ōita]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[ja:%E9%95%B7%E6%B5%9C%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E5%A4%A7%E5%88%86%E5%B8%82)]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[ja:&action=edit]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:images]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:長浜神社]]

{{Shrine | name = Nagahama Shrine (長浜神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 2.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | location = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)}} 1-8-7 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} | Shrine_rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | consecration_year = Tradition dates to {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}} 13 (1406 CE) | Main_building = | Other_name = Nagahama-sama[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2016/06/21/135546234 「雨の長浜さま」あれこれ【おおいた祭時記】] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, June 21, 2016 | Events = Annual festival: July 5–7 | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Nagahama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 1.jpg|thumb|right|Torii gate]] [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 3.jpg|thumb|right|Hina ema (wooden plaques)]] '''Nagahama Shrine''' (長浜神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}. The shrine's primary deity is {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}}[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2015/12/28/132221197 新年の舞「めおと」で 雌の獅子頭奉納 大分市の長浜神社] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, December 28, 2015, and it also enshrines {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}}[http://www.niigata-u.com/files/oita/nagahama.html Nagahama Shrine (Nagahama Town, Ōita City)] Ōita Shrine Exploration. Under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度}}, the shrine was classified as a gōsha. Nagahama Shrine is particularly revered by women for its association with the healing of gynecological ailments, as it enshrines deities regarded as patrons of medicine, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}[http://www.i-oita.net/spot/history_culture/oita/9182.html Nagahama Shrine] iNavi Ōita (Ōita Broadcasting System). Devotees traditionally offer wooden plaques (ema) depicting male and female hina dolls when making prayersKatsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 12: Around Nagahama" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita012a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, November 2, 2007. == History == According to shrine tradition, Nagahama Shrine was founded in {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}} 13 (1406). A local resident of Nagahama had a divine dream, and the following morning discovered a small wooden shrine box washed ashore, containing a miniature altar inscribed "Yoshu Nagahama Daimyōjin" on the inner side of its door and a sacred mirror inside. Upon reporting this discovery to {{ill|Ōtomo Chikayo|ja|大友親世}}, the {{ill|Governor of Bungo Province|ja|豊後国守護}}, Chikayo donated shrine land and funds for construction. The shrine was established and named "Nagahama Daimyōjin"Katsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 1: Around Shōwa Avenue" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, August 17, 2007. The shrine was damaged by the tsunami caused by the {{ill|Keichō Bungo earthquake|ja|慶長豊後地震}} on {{ill|July 12 (lunar calendar)|ja|7月12日 (旧暦)}} in {{ill|Bunroku|ja|文禄}} 5 (1596 CE). It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} 9 (1681 CE). In {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正}} 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from {{ill|July 5|ja|7月5日}} to {{ill|July 7|ja|7月7日}}, is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿}} processions, {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}, and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the {{ill|Fuchū domain (Bungo)|ja|府内藩}}, {{ill|Matsudaira Chikayoshi|ja|松平近説}}, was halted in 1922 but resumed in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of {{ill|Ligustrum japonicum|ja|ネズミモチ|lt=Privet}} adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōita Station|ja|大分駅}}, {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|九州旅客鉄道}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ōita]] [[Category:History of Ōita]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[ja:%E9%95%B7%E6%B5%9C%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E5%A4%A7%E5%88%86%E5%B8%82)]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[ja:&action=edit]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:images]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:長浜神社]]

Stems of {{ill|Ligustrum japonicum|ja|ネズミモチ|lt=Privet}} adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōita Station|ja|大分駅}}, {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|九州旅客鉄道}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ōita]] [[Category:History of Ōita]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[ja:%E9%95%B7%E6%B5%9C%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E5%A4%A7%E5%88%86%E5%B8%82)]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[ja:&action=edit]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:images]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:長浜神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Qqqqq]]

{{Shrine | name = Nagahama Shrine (長浜神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 2.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | location = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)}} 1-8-7 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} | Shrine_rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | consecration_year = Tradition dates to {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}} 13 (1406 CE) | Main_building = | Other_name = Nagahama-sama[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2016/06/21/135546234 「雨の長浜さま」あれこれ【おおいた祭時記】] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, June 21, 2016 | Events = Annual festival: July 5–7 | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Nagahama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 1.jpg|thumb|right|Torii gate]] [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 3.jpg|thumb|right|Hina ema (wooden plaques)]] '''Nagahama Shrine''' (長浜神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}. The shrine's primary deity is {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}}[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2015/12/28/132221197 新年の舞「めおと」で 雌の獅子頭奉納 大分市の長浜神社] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, December 28, 2015, and it also enshrines {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}}[http://www.niigata-u.com/files/oita/nagahama.html Nagahama Shrine (Nagahama Town, Ōita City)] Ōita Shrine Exploration. Under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度}}, the shrine was classified as a gōsha. Nagahama Shrine is particularly revered by women for its association with the healing of gynecological ailments, as it enshrines deities regarded as patrons of medicine, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}[http://www.i-oita.net/spot/history_culture/oita/9182.html Nagahama Shrine] iNavi Ōita (Ōita Broadcasting System). Devotees traditionally offer wooden plaques (ema) depicting male and female hina dolls when making prayersKatsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 12: Around Nagahama" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita012a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, November 2, 2007. == History == According to shrine tradition, Nagahama Shrine was founded in {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}} 13 (1406). A local resident of Nagahama had a divine dream, and the following morning discovered a small wooden shrine box washed ashore, containing a miniature altar inscribed "Yoshu Nagahama Daimyōjin" on the inner side of its door and a sacred mirror inside. Upon reporting this discovery to {{ill|Ōtomo Chikayo|ja|大友親世}}, the {{ill|Governor of Bungo Province|ja|豊後国守護}}, Chikayo donated shrine land and funds for construction. The shrine was established and named "Nagahama Daimyōjin"Katsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 1: Around Shōwa Avenue" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, August 17, 2007. The shrine was damaged by the tsunami caused by the {{ill|Keichō Bungo earthquake|ja|慶長豊後地震}} on {{ill|July 12 (lunar calendar)|ja|7月12日 (旧暦)}} in {{ill|Bunroku|ja|文禄}} 5 (1596 CE). It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} 9 (1681 CE). In {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正}} 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from {{ill|July 5|ja|7月5日}} to {{ill|July 7|ja|7月7日}}, is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿}} processions, {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}, and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the {{ill|Fuchū domain (Bungo)|ja|府内藩}}, {{ill|Matsudaira Chikayoshi|ja|松平近説}}, was halted in 1922 but resumed in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of {{ill|Ligustrum japonicum|ja|ネズミモチ|lt=Privet}} adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōita Station|ja|大分駅}}, {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|九州旅客鉄道}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ōita]] [[Category:History of Ōita]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[ja:%E9%95%B7%E6%B5%9C%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E5%A4%A7%E5%88%86%E5%B8%82)]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[ja:&action=edit]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:images]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:suQqqqqq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:長浜神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Qqqqq]]

The mikoshi procession, dating back to {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the {{ill|Fuchū domain (Bungo)|ja|府内藩}}, {{ill|Matsudaira Chikayoshi|ja|松平近説}}, was halted in 1922 but resumed in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of {{ill|Ligustrum japonicum|ja|ネズミモチ|lt=Privet}} adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōita Station|ja|大分駅}}, {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|九州旅客鉄道}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ōita]] [[Category:History of Ōita]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[ja:%E9%95%B7%E6%B5%9C%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E5%A4%A7%E5%88%86%E5%B8%82)]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[ja:&action=edit]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:images]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:suQqqqqq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:長浜神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Qqqqq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]]

{{Shrine | name = Nagahama Shrine (長浜神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 2.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | location = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)}} 1-8-7 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} | Shrine_rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | consecration_year = Tradition dates to {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}} 13 (1406 CE) | Main_building = | Other_name = Nagahama-sama[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2016/06/21/135546234 「雨の長浜さま」あれこれ【おおいた祭時記】] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, June 21, 2016 | Events = Annual festival: July 5–7 | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Nagahama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 1.jpg|thumb|right|Torii gate]] [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 3.jpg|thumb|right|Hina ema (wooden plaques)]] '''Nagahama Shrine''' (長浜神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}. The shrine's primary deity is {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}}[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2015/12/28/132221197 新年の舞「めおと」で 雌の獅子頭奉納 大分市の長浜神社] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, December 28, 2015, and it also enshrines {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}}[http://www.niigata-u.com/files/oita/nagahama.html Nagahama Shrine (Nagahama Town, Ōita City)] Ōita Shrine Exploration. Under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度}}, the shrine was classified as a gōsha. Nagahama Shrine is particularly revered by women for its association with the healing of gynecological ailments, as it enshrines deities regarded as patrons of medicine, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}[http://www.i-oita.net/spot/history_culture/oita/9182.html Nagahama Shrine] iNavi Ōita (Ōita Broadcasting System). Devotees traditionally offer wooden plaques (ema) depicting male and female hina dolls when making prayersKatsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 12: Around Nagahama" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita012a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, November 2, 2007. == History == According to shrine tradition, Nagahama Shrine was founded in {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}} 13 (1406). A local resident of Nagahama had a divine dream, and the following morning discovered a small wooden shrine box washed ashore, containing a miniature altar inscribed "Yoshu Nagahama Daimyōjin" on the inner side of its door and a sacred mirror inside. Upon reporting this discovery to {{ill|Ōtomo Chikayo|ja|大友親世}}, the {{ill|Governor of Bungo Province|ja|豊後国守護}}, Chikayo donated shrine land and funds for construction. The shrine was established and named "Nagahama Daimyōjin"Katsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 1: Around Shōwa Avenue" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, August 17, 2007. The shrine was damaged by the tsunami caused by the {{ill|Keichō Bungo earthquake|ja|慶長豊後地震}} on {{ill|July 12 (lunar calendar)|ja|7月12日 (旧暦)}} in {{ill|Bunroku|ja|文禄}} 5 (1596 CE). It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} 9 (1681 CE). In {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正}} 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from {{ill|July 5|ja|7月5日}} to {{ill|July 7|ja|7月7日}}, is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿}} processions, {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}, and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the {{ill|Fuchū domain (Bungo)|ja|府内藩}}, {{ill|Matsudaira Chikayoshi|ja|松平近説}}, was halted in 1922 but resumed in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of {{ill|Ligustrum japonicum|ja|ネズミモチ|lt=Privet}} adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōita Station|ja|大分駅}}, {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|九州旅客鉄道}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ōita]] [[Category:History of Ōita]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[ja:%E9%95%B7%E6%B5%9C%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E5%A4%A7%E5%88%86%E5%B8%82)]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[ja:&action=edit]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:images]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:suQqqqqq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:長浜神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Qqqqq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:New]]

It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿}} processions, {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}, and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the {{ill|Fuchū domain (Bungo)|ja|府内藩}}, {{ill|Matsudaira Chikayoshi|ja|松平近説}}, was halted in 1922 but resumed in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of {{ill|Ligustrum japonicum|ja|ネズミモチ|lt=Privet}} adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōita Station|ja|大分駅}}, {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|九州旅客鉄道}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ōita]] [[Category:History of Ōita]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:Unsorted Completion categories]] [[ja:%E9%95%B7%E6%B5%9C%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E5%A4%A7%E5%88%86%E5%B8%82)]][[Category:Unsorted Completion categories]] [[ja:&action=edit]] [[Category:Unsorted Completion categories]] [[Category:images]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:suQqqqqq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:長浜神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Qqqqq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]][[Category:tier 4]] [[Category:New]]

{{Shrine | name = Nagahama Shrine (長浜神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 2.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | location = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)}} 1-8-7 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} | Shrine_rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | consecration_year = Tradition dates to {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}} 13 (1406 CE) | Main_building = | Other_name = Nagahama-sama[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2016/06/21/135546234 「雨の長浜さま」あれこれ【おおいた祭時記】] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, June 21, 2016 | Events = Annual festival: July 5–7 | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Nagahama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 1.jpg|thumb|right|Torii gate]] [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 3.jpg|thumb|right|Hina ema (wooden plaques)]] '''Nagahama Shrine''' (長浜神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}. The shrine's primary deity is {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}}[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2015/12/28/132221197 新年の舞「めおと」で 雌の獅子頭奉納 大分市の長浜神社] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, December 28, 2015, and it also enshrines {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}}[http://www.niigata-u.com/files/oita/nagahama.html Nagahama Shrine (Nagahama Town, Ōita City)] Ōita Shrine Exploration. Under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度}}, the shrine was classified as a gōsha. Nagahama Shrine is particularly revered by women for its association with the healing of gynecological ailments, as it enshrines deities regarded as patrons of medicine, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}[http://www.i-oita.net/spot/history_culture/oita/9182.html Nagahama Shrine] iNavi Ōita (Ōita Broadcasting System). Devotees traditionally offer wooden plaques (ema) depicting male and female hina dolls when making prayersKatsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 12: Around Nagahama" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita012a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, November 2, 2007. == History == According to shrine tradition, Nagahama Shrine was founded in {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}} 13 (1406). A local resident of Nagahama had a divine dream, and the following morning discovered a small wooden shrine box washed ashore, containing a miniature altar inscribed "Yoshu Nagahama Daimyōjin" on the inner side of its door and a sacred mirror inside. Upon reporting this discovery to {{ill|Ōtomo Chikayo|ja|大友親世}}, the {{ill|Governor of Bungo Province|ja|豊後国守護}}, Chikayo donated shrine land and funds for construction. The shrine was established and named "Nagahama Daimyōjin"Katsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 1: Around Shōwa Avenue" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, August 17, 2007. The shrine was damaged by the tsunami caused by the {{ill|Keichō Bungo earthquake|ja|慶長豊後地震}} on {{ill|July 12 (lunar calendar)|ja|7月12日 (旧暦)}} in {{ill|Bunroku|ja|文禄}} 5 (1596 CE). It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} 9 (1681 CE). In {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正}} 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from {{ill|July 5|ja|7月5日}} to {{ill|July 7|ja|7月7日}}, is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿}} processions, {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}, and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the {{ill|Fuchū domain (Bungo)|ja|府内藩}}, {{ill|Matsudaira Chikayoshi|ja|松平近説}}, was halted in 1922 but resumed in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of {{ill|Ligustrum japonicum|ja|ネズミモチ|lt=Privet}} adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōita Station|ja|大分駅}}, {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|九州旅客鉄道}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ōita]] [[Category:History of Ōita]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:U]][[Category:c]][[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category: C]][[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: categories]] [[ja:%E9%95%B7%E6%B5%9C%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E5%A4%A7%E5%88%86%E5%B8%82)]][[Category:U]][[Category:c]][[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category: C]][[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: categories]] [[ja:&action=edit]] [[Category:U]][[Category:c]][[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category: C]][[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: categories]] [[Category:images]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:suQqqqqq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:長浜神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Qqqqq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:C]][[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: tier 4]][[Category:tier 4]] [[Category:New]]

Devotees traditionally offer wooden plaques (ema) depicting male and female hina dolls when making prayersKatsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 12: Around Nagahama" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita012a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, November 2, 2007. == History == According to shrine tradition, Nagahama Shrine was founded in {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}} 13 (1406). A local resident of Nagahama had a divine dream, and the following morning discovered a small wooden shrine box washed ashore, containing a miniature altar inscribed "Yoshu Nagahama Daimyōjin" on the inner side of its door and a sacred mirror inside. Upon reporting this discovery to {{ill|Ōtomo Chikayo|ja|大友親世}}, the {{ill|Governor of Bungo Province|ja|豊後国守護}}, Chikayo donated shrine land and funds for construction. The shrine was established and named "Nagahama Daimyōjin"Katsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 1: Around Shōwa Avenue" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, August 17, 2007. The shrine was damaged by the tsunami caused by the {{ill|Keichō Bungo earthquake|ja|慶長豊後地震}} on {{ill|July 12 (lunar calendar)|ja|7月12日 (旧暦)}} in {{ill|Bunroku|ja|文禄}} 5 (1596 CE). It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} 9 (1681 CE). In {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正}} 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from {{ill|July 5|ja|7月5日}} to {{ill|July 7|ja|7月7日}}, is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿}} processions, {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}, and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the {{ill|Fuchū domain (Bungo)|ja|府内藩}}, {{ill|Matsudaira Chikayoshi|ja|松平近説}}, was halted in 1922 but resumed in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of {{ill|Ligustrum japonicum|ja|ネズミモチ|lt=Privet}} adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōita Station|ja|大分駅}}, {{ill|JR {{Shrine | name = Nagahama Shrine (長浜神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 2.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | location = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)}} 1-8-7 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} | Shrine_rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | consecration_year = Tradition dates to {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}} 13 (1406 CE) | Main_building = | Other_name = Nagahama-sama[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2016/06/21/135546234 「雨の長浜さま」あれこれ【おおいた祭時記】] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, June 21, 2016 | Events = Annual festival: July 5–7 | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Nagahama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 1.jpg|thumb|right|Torii gate]] [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 3.jpg|thumb|right|Hina ema (wooden plaques)]] '''Nagahama Shrine''' (長浜神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}. The shrine's primary deity is {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}}[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2015/12/28/132221197 新年の舞「めおと」で 雌の獅子頭奉納 大分市の長浜神社] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, December 28, 2015, and it also enshrines {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}}[http://www.niigata-u.com/files/oita/nagahama.html Nagahama Shrine (Nagahama Town, Ōita City)] Ōita Shrine Exploration. Under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度}}, the shrine was classified as a gōsha. Nagahama Shrine is particularly revered by women for its association with the healing of gynecological ailments, as it enshrines deities regarded as patrons of medicine, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}[http://www.i-oita.net/spot/history_culture/oita/9182.html Nagahama Shrine] iNavi Ōita (Ōita Broadcasting System). Devotees traditionally offer wooden plaques (ema) depicting male and female hina dolls when making prayersKatsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 12: Around Nagahama" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita012a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, November 2, 2007. == History == According to shrine tradition, Nagahama Shrine was founded in {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}} 13 (1406). A local resident of Nagahama had a divine dream, and the following morning discovered a small wooden shrine box washed ashore, containing a miniature altar inscribed "Yoshu Nagahama Daimyōjin" on the inner side of its door and a sacred mirror inside. Upon reporting this discovery to {{ill|Ōtomo Chikayo|ja|大友親世}}, the {{ill|Governor of Bungo Province|ja|豊後国守護}}, Chikayo donated shrine land and funds for construction. The shrine was established and named "Nagahama Daimyōjin"Katsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 1: Around Shōwa Avenue" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, August 17, 2007. The shrine was damaged by the tsunami caused by the {{ill|Keichō Bungo earthquake|ja|慶長豊後地震}} on {{ill|July 12 (lunar calendar)|ja|7月12日 (旧暦)}} in {{ill|Bunroku|ja|文禄}} 5 (1596 CE). It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} 9 (1681 CE). In {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正}} 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from {{ill|July 5|ja|7月5日}} to {{ill|July 7|ja|7月7日}}, is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿}} processions, {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}, and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the {{ill|Fuchū domain (Bungo)|ja|府内藩}}, {{ill|Matsudaira Chikayoshi|ja|松平近説}}, was halted in 1922 but resumed in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of {{ill|Ligustrum japonicum|ja|ネズミモチ|lt=Privet}} adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōita Station|ja|大分駅}}, {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|九州旅客鉄道}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ōita]] [[Category:History of Ōita]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]] {{Shrine | name = Nagahama Shrine (長浜神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 2.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | location = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)}} 1-8-7 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} | Shrine_rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | consecration_year = Tradition dates to {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}} 13 (1406 CE) | Main_building = | Other_name = Nagahama-sama[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2016/06/21/135546234 「雨の長浜さま」あれこれ【おおいた祭時記】] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, June 21, 2016 | Events = Annual festival: July 5–7 | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Nagahama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 1.jpg|thumb|right|Torii gate]] [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 3.jpg|thumb|right|Hina ema (wooden plaques)]] '''Nagahama Shrine''' (長浜神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}. The shrine's primary deity is {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}}[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2015/12/28/132221197 新年の舞「めおと」で 雌の獅子頭奉納 大分市の長浜神社] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, December 28, 2015, and it also enshrines {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}}[http://www.niigata-u.com/files/oita/nagahama.html Nagahama Shrine (Nagahama Town, Ōita City)] Ōita Shrine Exploration. Under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度}}, the shrine was classified as a gōsha. Nagahama Shrine is particularly revered by women for its association with the healing of gynecological ailments, as it enshrines deities regarded as patrons of medicine, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}[http://www.i-oita.net/spot/history_culture/oita/9182.html Nagahama Shrine] iNavi Ōita (Ōita Broadcasting System). Devotees traditionally offer wooden plaques (ema) depicting male and female hina dolls when making prayersKatsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 12: Around Nagahama" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita012a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, November 2, 2007. == History == According to shrine tradition, Nagahama Shrine was founded in {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}} 13 (1406). A local resident of Nagahama had a divine dream, and the following morning discovered a small wooden shrine box washed ashore, containing a miniature altar inscribed "Yoshu Nagahama Daimyōjin" on the inner side of its door and a sacred mirror inside. Upon reporting this discovery to {{ill|Ōtomo Chikayo|ja|大友親世}}, the {{ill|Governor of Bungo Province|ja|豊後国守護}}, Chikayo donated shrine land and funds for construction. The shrine was established and named "Nagahama Daimyōjin"Katsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 1: Around Shōwa Avenue" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, August 17, 2007. The shrine was damaged by the tsunami caused by the {{ill|Keichō Bungo earthquake|ja|慶長豊後地震}} on {{ill|July 12 (lunar calendar)|ja|7月12日 (旧暦)}} in {{ill|Bunroku|ja|文禄}} 5 (1596 CE). It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} 9 (1681 CE). In {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正}} 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from {{ill|July 5|ja|7月5日}} to {{ill|July 7|ja|7月7日}}, is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿}} processions, {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}, and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the {{ill|Fuchū domain (Bungo)|ja|府内藩}}, {{ill|Matsudaira Chikayoshi|ja|松平近説}}, was halted in 1922 but resumed in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of {{ill|Ligustrum japonicum|ja|ネズミモチ|lt=Privet}} adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōita Station|ja|大分駅}}, {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|九州旅客鉄道}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ōita]] [[Category:History of Ōita]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]] It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} 9 (1681 CE). In {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正}} 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from {{ill|July 5|ja|7月5日}} to {{ill|July 7|ja|7月7日}}, is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿}} processions, {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}, and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the {{ill|Fuchū domain (Bungo)|ja|府内藩}}, {{ill|Matsudaira Chikayoshi|ja|松平近説}}, was halted in 1922 but resumed in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of {{ill|Ligustrum japonicum|ja|ネズミモチ|lt=Privet}} adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōita Station|ja|大分駅}}, {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|九州旅客鉄道}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and {{Shrine | name = Nagahama Shrine (長浜神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 2.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | location = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)}} 1-8-7 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} | Shrine_rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | consecration_year = Tradition dates to {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}} 13 (1406 CE) | Main_building = | Other_name = Nagahama-sama[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2016/06/21/135546234 「雨の長浜さま」あれこれ【おおいた祭時記】] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, June 21, 2016 | Events = Annual festival: July 5–7 | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Nagahama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 1.jpg|thumb|right|Torii gate]] [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 3.jpg|thumb|right|Hina ema (wooden plaques)]] '''Nagahama Shrine''' (長浜神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}. The shrine's primary deity is {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}}[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2015/12/28/132221197 新年の舞「めおと」で 雌の獅子頭奉納 大分市の長浜神社] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, December 28, 2015, and it also enshrines {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}}[http://www.niigata-u.com/files/oita/nagahama.html Nagahama Shrine (Nagahama Town, Ōita City)] Ōita Shrine Exploration. Under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度}}, the shrine was classified as a gōsha. Nagahama Shrine is particularly revered by women for its association with the healing of gynecological ailments, as it enshrines deities regarded as patrons of medicine, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}[http://www.i-oita.net/spot/history_culture/oita/9182.html Nagahama Shrine] iNavi Ōita (Ōita Broadcasting System). Devotees traditionally offer wooden plaques (ema) depicting male and female hina dolls when making prayersKatsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 12: Around Nagahama" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita012a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, November 2, 2007. == History == According to shrine tradition, Nagahama Shrine was founded in {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}} 13 (1406). A local resident of Nagahama had a divine dream, and the following morning discovered a small wooden shrine box washed ashore, containing a miniature altar inscribed "Yoshu Nagahama Daimyōjin" on the inner side of its door and a sacred mirror inside. Upon reporting this discovery to {{ill|Ōtomo Chikayo|ja|大友親世}}, the {{ill|Governor of Bungo Province|ja|豊後国守護}}, Chikayo donated shrine land and funds for construction. The shrine was established and named "Nagahama Daimyōjin"Katsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 1: Around Shōwa Avenue" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, August 17, 2007. The shrine was damaged by the tsunami caused by the {{ill|Keichō Bungo earthquake|ja|慶長豊後地震}} on {{ill|July 12 (lunar calendar)|ja|7月12日 (旧暦)}} in {{ill|Bunroku|ja|文禄}} 5 (1596 CE). It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} 9 (1681 CE). In {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正}} 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from {{ill|July 5|ja|7月5日}} to {{ill|July 7|ja|7月7日}}, is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿}} processions, {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}, and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the {{ill|Fuchū domain (Bungo)|ja|府内藩}}, {{ill|Matsudaira Chikayoshi|ja|松平近説}}, was halted in 1922 but resumed in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of {{ill|Ligustrum japonicum|ja|ネズミモチ|lt=Privet}} adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōita Station|ja|大分駅}}, {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|九州旅客鉄道}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and {{Shrine | name = Nagahama Shrine (長浜神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 2.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | location = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)}} 1-8-7 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} | Shrine_rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | consecration_year = Tradition dates to {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}} 13 (1406 CE) | Main_building = | Other_name = Nagahama-sama[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2016/06/21/135546234 「雨の長浜さま」あれこれ【おおいた祭時記】] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, June 21, 2016 | Events = Annual festival: July 5–7 | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Nagahama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 1.jpg|thumb|right|Torii gate]] [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 3.jpg|thumb|right|Hina ema (wooden plaques)]] '''Nagahama Shrine''' (長浜神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}. The shrine's primary deity is {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}}[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2015/12/28/132221197 新年の舞「めおと」で 雌の獅子頭奉納 大分市の長浜神社] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, December 28, 2015, and it also enshrines {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}}[http://www.niigata-u.com/files/oita/nagahama.html Nagahama Shrine (Nagahama Town, Ōita City)] Ōita Shrine Exploration. Under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, the shrine was classified as a gōsha. Nagahama Shrine is particularly revered by women for its association with the healing of gynecological ailments, as it enshrines deities regarded as patrons of medicine, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}[http://www.i-oita.net/spot/history_culture/oita/9182.html Nagahama Shrine] iNavi Ōita (Ōita Broadcasting System). Devotees traditionally offer wooden plaques (ema) depicting male and female hina dolls when making prayersKatsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 12: Around Nagahama" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita012a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, November 2, 2007. == History == According to shrine tradition, Nagahama Shrine was founded in {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}} 13 (1406). A local resident of Nagahama had a divine dream, and the following morning discovered a small wooden shrine box washed ashore, containing a miniature altar inscribed "Yoshu Nagahama Daimyōjin" on the inner side of its door and a sacred mirror inside. Upon reporting this discovery to {{ill|Ōtomo Chikayo|ja|大友親世}}, the {{ill|Governor of Bungo Province|ja|豊後国守護}}, Chikayo donated shrine land and funds for construction. The shrine was established and named "Nagahama Daimyōjin"Katsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 1: Around Shōwa Avenue" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, August 17, 2007. The shrine was damaged by the tsunami caused by the {{ill|Keichō Bungo earthquake|ja|慶長豊後地震}} on {{ill|July 12 (lunar calendar)|ja|7月12日 (旧暦)}} in {{ill|Bunroku|ja|文禄}} 5 (1596 CE). It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} 9 (1681 CE). In {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正}} 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from {{ill|July 5|ja|7月5日}} to {{ill|July 7|ja|7月7日}}, is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿}} processions, {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}, and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the {{ill|Fuchū domain (Bungo)|ja|府内藩}}, {{ill|Matsudaira Chikayoshi|ja|松平近説}}, was halted in 1922 but resumed in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of {{ill|Ligustrum japonicum|ja|ネズミモチ|lt=Privet}} adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōita Station|ja|大分駅}}, {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|九州旅客鉄道}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ōita]] [[Category:History of Ōita]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:11]] (Ōita)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:11]] [[ja:長浜神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:New]]
