{{Shrine | name = Nagahama Shrine (長浜神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 2.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | location = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)}} 1-8-7 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} | Shrine_rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | consecration_year = Tradition dates to {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}} 13 (1406 CE) | Main_building = | Other_name = Nagahama-sama[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2016/06/21/135546234 「雨の長浜さま」あれこれ【おおいた祭時記】] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, June 21, 2016 | Events = Annual festival: July 5–7 | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Nagahama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 1.jpg|thumb|right|Torii gate]] [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 3.jpg|thumb|right|Hina ema (wooden plaques)]] '''Nagahama Shrine''' (長浜神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}. The shrine's primary deity is {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}}[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2015/12/28/132221197 新年の舞「めおと」で 雌の獅子頭奉納 大分市の長浜神社] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, December 28, 2015, and it also enshrines {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane}}[http://www.niigata-u.com/files/oita/nagahama.html Nagahama Shrine (Nagahama Town, Ōita City)] Ōita Shrine Exploration. Under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, the shrine was classified as a gōsha. Nagahama Shrine is particularly revered by women for its association with the healing of gynecological ailments, as it enshrines deities regarded as patrons of medicine, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}[http://www.i-oita.net/spot/history_culture/oita/9182.html Nagahama Shrine] iNavi Ōita (Ōita Broadcasting System). Devotees traditionally offer wooden plaques (ema) depicting male and female hina dolls when making prayersKatsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 12: Around Nagahama" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita012a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, November 2, 2007. == History == According to shrine tradition, Nagahama Shrine was founded in {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}} 13 (1406). A local resident of Nagahama had a divine dream, and the following morning discovered a small wooden shrine box washed ashore, containing a miniature altar inscribed "Yoshu Nagahama Daimyōjin" on the inner side of its door and a sacred mirror inside. Upon reporting this discovery to {{ill|Ōtomo Chikayo|ja|大友親世}}, the {{ill|Governor of Bungo Province|ja|豊後国守護}}, Chikayo donated shrine land and funds for construction. The shrine was established and named "Nagahama Daimyōjin"Katsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 1: Around Shōwa Avenue" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, August 17, 2007. The shrine was damaged by the tsunami caused by the {{ill|Keichō Bungo earthquake|ja|慶長豊後地震}} on {{ill|July 12 (lunar calendar)|ja|7月12日 (旧暦)}} in {{ill|Bunroku|ja|文禄}} 5 (1596 CE). It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} 9 (1681 CE). In {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正}} 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from {{ill|July 5|ja|7月5日}} to {{ill|July 7|ja|7月7日}}, is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿}} processions, {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}, and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the {{ill|Fuchū domain (Bungo)|ja|府内藩}}, {{ill|Matsudaira Chikayoshi|ja|松平近説}}, was halted in 1922 but resumed in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of {{ill|Ligustrum japonicum|ja|ネズミモチ|lt=Privet}} adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōita Station|ja|大分駅}}, {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|九州旅客鉄道}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and The shrine was established and named "Nagahama Daimyōjin"Katsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 1: Around Shōwa Avenue" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, August 17, 2007. The shrine was damaged by the tsunami caused by the {{ill|Keichō Bungo earthquake|ja|慶長豊後地震}} on {{ill|July 12 (lunar calendar)|ja|7月12日 (旧暦)}} in {{ill|Bunroku|ja|文禄}} 5 (1596 CE). It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} 9 (1681 CE). In {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正}} 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from {{ill|July 5|ja|7月5日}} to {{ill|July 7|ja|7月7日}}, is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿}} processions, {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}, and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the {{ill|Fuchū domain (Bungo)|ja|府内藩}}, {{ill|Matsudaira Chikayoshi|ja|松平近説}}, was halted in 1922 but resumed in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of {{ill|Ligustrum japonicum|ja|ネズミモチ|lt=Privet}} adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōita Station|ja|大分駅}}, {{ill|JR The shrine's primary deity is {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}}[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2015/12/28/132221197 新年の舞「めおと」で 雌の獅子頭奉納 大分市の長浜神社] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, December 28, 2015, and it also enshrines {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane}}[http://www.niigata-u.com/files/oita/nagahama.html Nagahama Shrine (Nagahama Town, Ōita City)] Ōita Shrine Exploration. {{Shrine | name = Nagahama Shrine (長浜神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 2.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | location = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)}} 1-8-7 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} | Shrine_rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | consecration_year = Tradition dates to {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}} 13 (1406 CE) | Main_building = | Other_name = Nagahama-sama[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2016/06/21/135546234 「雨の長浜さま」あれこれ【おおいた祭時記】] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, June 21, 2016 | Events = Annual festival: July 5–7 | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Nagahama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 1.jpg|thumb|right|Torii gate]] [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 3.jpg|thumb|right|Hina ema (wooden plaques)]] '''Nagahama Shrine''' (長浜神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}. The shrine's primary deity is {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}}[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2015/12/28/132221197 新年の舞「めおと」で 雌の獅子頭奉納 大分市の長浜神社] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, December 28, 2015, and it also enshrines {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane}}[http://www.niigata-u.com/files/oita/nagahama.html Nagahama Shrine (Nagahama Town, Ōita City)] Ōita Shrine Exploration. Under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, the shrine was classified as a gōsha. Nagahama Shrine is particularly revered by women for its association with the healing of gynecological ailments, as it enshrines deities regarded as patrons of medicine, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}[http://www.i-oita.net/spot/history_culture/oita/9182.html Nagahama Shrine] iNavi Ōita (Ōita Broadcasting System). Devotees traditionally offer wooden plaques (ema) depicting male and female hina dolls when making prayersKatsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 12: Around Nagahama" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita012a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, November 2, 2007. == History == According to shrine tradition, Nagahama Shrine was founded in {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}} 13 (1406). A local resident of Nagahama had a divine dream, and the following morning discovered a small wooden shrine box washed ashore, containing a miniature altar inscribed "Yoshu Nagahama Daimyōjin" on the inner side of its door and a sacred mirror inside. Upon reporting this discovery to {{ill|Ōtomo Chikayo|ja|大友親世}}, the {{ill|Governor of Bungo Province|ja|豊後国守護}}, Chikayo donated shrine land and funds for construction. The shrine was established and named "Nagahama Daimyōjin"Katsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 1: Around Shōwa Avenue" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, August 17, 2007. The shrine was damaged by the tsunami caused by the {{ill|Keichō Bungo earthquake|ja|慶長豊後地震}} on {{ill|July 12 (lunar calendar)|ja|7月12日 (旧暦)}} in {{ill|Bunroku|ja|文禄}} 5 (1596 CE). It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} 9 (1681 CE). In {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正}} 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from {{ill|July 5|ja|7月5日}} to {{ill|July 7|ja|7月7日}}, is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿}} processions, {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}, and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the {{ill|Fuchū domain (Bungo)|ja|府内藩}}, {{ill|Matsudaira Chikayoshi|ja|松平近説}}, was halted in 1922 but resumed in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of {{ill|Ligustrum japonicum|ja|ネズミモチ|lt=Privet}} adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōita Station|ja|大分駅}}, {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|九州旅客鉄道}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and {{Shrine | name = Nagahama Shrine (長浜神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 2.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | location = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)}} 1-8-7 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} | Shrine_rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | consecration_year = Tradition dates to {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}} 13 (1406 CE) | Main_building = | Other_name = Nagahama-sama[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2016/06/21/135546234 「雨の長浜さま」あれこれ【おおいた祭時記】] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, June 21, 2016 | Events = Annual festival: July 5–7 | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Nagahama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 1.jpg|thumb|right|Torii gate]] [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 3.jpg|thumb|right|Hina ema (wooden plaques)]] '''Nagahama Shrine''' (長浜神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}. The shrine's primary deity is {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}}[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2015/12/28/132221197 新年の舞「めおと」で 雌の獅子頭奉納 大分市の長浜神社] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, December 28, 2015, and it also enshrines {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane}}[http://www.niigata-u.com/files/oita/nagahama.html Nagahama Shrine (Nagahama Town, Ōita City)] Ōita Shrine Exploration. Under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, the shrine was classified as a gōsha. Nagahama Shrine is particularly revered by women for its association with the healing of gynecological ailments, as it enshrines deities regarded as patrons of medicine, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}[http://www.i-oita.net/spot/history_culture/oita/9182.html Nagahama Shrine] iNavi Ōita (Ōita Broadcasting System). Devotees traditionally offer wooden plaques (ema) depicting male and female hina dolls when making prayersKatsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 12: Around Nagahama" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita012a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, November 2, 2007. == History == According to shrine tradition, Nagahama Shrine was founded in {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}} 13 (1406). A local resident of Nagahama had a divine dream, and the following morning discovered a small wooden shrine box washed ashore, containing a miniature altar inscribed "Yoshu Nagahama Daimyōjin" on the inner side of its door and a sacred mirror inside. Upon reporting this discovery to {{ill|Ōtomo Chikayo|ja|大友親世}}, the {{ill|Governor of Bungo Province|ja|豊後国守護}}, Chikayo donated shrine land and funds for construction. The shrine was established and named "Nagahama Daimyōjin"Katsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 1: Around Shōwa Avenue" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, August 17, 2007. The shrine was damaged by the tsunami caused by the {{ill|Keichō Bungo earthquake|ja|慶長豊後地震}} on {{ill|July 12 (lunar calendar)|ja|7月12日 (旧暦)}} in {{ill|Bunroku|ja|文禄}} 5 (1596 CE). It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} 9 (1681 CE). In {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正}} 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from {{ill|July 5|ja|7月5日}} to {{ill|July 7|ja|7月7日}}, is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿}} processions, {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}, and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the {{ill|Fuchū domain (Bungo)|ja|府内藩}}, {{ill|Matsudaira Chikayoshi|ja|松平近説}}, was halted in 1922 but resumed in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of {{ill|Ligustrum japonicum|ja|ネズミモチ|lt=Privet}} adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōita Station|ja|大分駅}}, {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|九州旅客鉄道}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ōita]] [[Category:History of Ōita]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines]] [[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: tier 4]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: tier 4]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category:11]] Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:11]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)}}

Nagahama Shrine is particularly revered by women for its association with the healing of gynecological ailments, as it enshrines deities regarded as patrons of medicine, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}[http://www.i-oita.net/spot/history_culture/oita/9182.html Nagahama Shrine] iNavi Ōita (Ōita Broadcasting System). Devotees traditionally offer wooden plaques (ema) depicting male and female hina dolls when making prayersKatsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 12: Around Nagahama" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita012a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, November 2, 2007. == History == According to shrine tradition, Nagahama Shrine was founded in {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}} 13 (1406). A local resident of Nagahama had a divine dream, and the following morning discovered a small wooden shrine box washed ashore, containing a miniature altar inscribed "Yoshu Nagahama Daimyōjin" on the inner side of its door and a sacred mirror inside. Upon reporting this discovery to {{ill|Ōtomo Chikayo|ja|大友親世}}, the {{ill|Governor of Bungo Province|ja|豊後国守護}}, Chikayo donated shrine land and funds for construction. The shrine was established and named "Nagahama Daimyōjin"Katsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 1: Around Shōwa Avenue" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, August 17, 2007. The shrine was damaged by the tsunami caused by the {{ill|Keichō Bungo earthquake|ja|慶長豊後地震}} on {{ill|July 12 (lunar calendar)|ja|7月12日 (旧暦)}} in {{ill|Bunroku|ja|文禄}} 5 (1596 CE). It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} 9 (1681 CE). In {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正}} 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from {{ill|July 5|ja|7月5日}} to {{ill|July 7|ja|7月7日}}, is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿}} processions, {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}, and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the {{ill|Fuchū domain (Bungo)|ja|府内藩}}, {{ill|Matsudaira Chikayoshi|ja|松平近説}}, was halted in 1922 but resumed in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of {{ill|Ligustrum japonicum|ja|ネズミモチ|lt=Privet}} adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōita Station|ja|大分駅}}, {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|九州旅客鉄道}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and Under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, the shrine was classified as a gōsha. Nagahama Shrine is particularly revered by women for its association with the healing of gynecological ailments, as it enshrines deities regarded as patrons of medicine, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}[http://www.i-oita.net/spot/history_culture/oita/9182.html Nagahama Shrine] iNavi Ōita (Ōita Broadcasting System). Upon reporting this discovery to {{ill|Ōtomo Chikayo|ja|大友親世}}, the {{ill|Governor of Bungo Province|ja|豊後国守護}}, Chikayo donated shrine land and funds for construction. It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." {{Shrine | name = Nagahama Shrine (長浜神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 2.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | location = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)}} 1-8-7 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona}} | Shrine_rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | consecration_year = Tradition dates to {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}} 13 (1406 CE) | Main_building = | Other_name = Nagahama-sama[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2016/06/21/135546234 「雨の長浜さま」あれこれ【おおいた祭時記】] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, June 21, 2016 | Events = Annual festival: July 5–7 | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Nagahama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 1.jpg|thumb|right|Torii gate]] [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 3.jpg|thumb|right|Hina ema (wooden plaques)]] '''Nagahama Shrine''' (長浜神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}. The shrine's primary deity is {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona}}[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2015/12/28/132221197 新年の舞「めおと」で 雌の獅子頭奉納 大分市の長浜神社] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, December 28, 2015, and it also enshrines {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane}}[http://www.niigata-u.com/files/oita/nagahama.html Nagahama Shrine (Nagahama Town, Ōita City)] Ōita Shrine Exploration. Under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, the shrine was classified as a gōsha. Nagahama Shrine is particularly revered by women for its association with the healing of gynecological ailments, as it enshrines deities regarded as patrons of medicine, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}[http://www.i-oita.net/spot/history_culture/oita/9182.html Nagahama Shrine] iNavi Ōita (Ōita Broadcasting System). Devotees traditionally offer wooden plaques (ema) depicting male and female hina dolls when making prayersKatsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 12: Around Nagahama" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita012a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, November 2, 2007. == History == According to shrine tradition, Nagahama Shrine was founded in {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}} 13 (1406). A local resident of Nagahama had a divine dream, and the following morning discovered a small wooden shrine box washed ashore, containing a miniature altar inscribed "Yoshu Nagahama Daimyōjin" on the inner side of its door and a sacred mirror inside. Upon reporting this discovery to {{ill|Ōtomo Chikayo|ja|大友親世}}, the {{ill|Governor of Bungo Province|ja|豊後国守護}}, Chikayo donated shrine land and funds for construction. The shrine was established and named "Nagahama Daimyōjin"Katsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 1: Around Shōwa Avenue" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, August 17, 2007. The shrine was damaged by the tsunami caused by the {{ill|Keichō Bungo earthquake|ja|慶長豊後地震}} on {{ill|July 12 (lunar calendar)|ja|7月12日 (旧暦)}} in {{ill|Bunroku|ja|文禄}} 5 (1596 CE). It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)|1=Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} 9 (1681 CE). In {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正}} 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from {{ill|July 5|ja|7月5日}} to {{ill|July 7|ja|7月7日}}, is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿}} processions, {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}, and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the {{ill|Fuchū domain (Bungo)|ja|府内藩}}, {{ill|Matsudaira Chikayoshi|ja|松平近説}}, was halted in 1922 but resumed in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of {{ill|Ligustrum japonicum|ja|ネズミモチ|lt=Privet}} adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōita Station|ja|大分駅}}, {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|九州旅客鉄道}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ōita]] [[Category:History of Ōita]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines]] [[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category: tier 4]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:completion tier (Ōita)}} [[Category:New]]

The shrine was damaged by the tsunami caused by the {{ill|Keichō Bungo earthquake|ja|慶長豊後地震}} on {{ill|July 12 (lunar calendar)|ja|7月12日 (旧暦)}} in {{ill|Bunroku|ja|文禄}} 5 (1596 CE). It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)|1=Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} 9 (1681 CE). In {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正}} 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from {{ill|July 5|ja|7月5日}} to {{ill|July 7|ja|7月7日}}, is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿}} processions, {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}, and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the {{ill|Fuchū domain (Bungo)|ja|府内藩}}, {{ill|Matsudaira Chikayoshi|ja|松平近説}}, was halted in 1922 but resumed in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of {{ill|Ligustrum japonicum|ja|ネズミモチ|lt=Privet}} adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōita Station|ja|大分駅}}, {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|九州旅客鉄道}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ōita]] [[Category:History of Ōita]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines]] [[Category:completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]] [[ja:%E9%95%B7%E6%B5%9C%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E5%A4%A7%E5%88%86%E5%B8%82)]] [[ja:&action=edit]] [[ja:長浜神社]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)}}

{{Shrine | name = Nagahama Shrine (長浜神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 2.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | location = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)|12=draft}} 1-8-7 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=draft}} | Shrine_rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|12=draft}} | consecration_year = Tradition dates to {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永|12=draft}} 13 (1406 CE) | Main_building = | Other_name = Nagahama-sama[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2016/06/21/135546234 「雨の長浜さま」あれこれ【おおいた祭時記】] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, June 21, 2016 | Events = Annual festival: July 5–7 | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Nagahama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 1.jpg|thumb|right|Torii gate]] [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 3.jpg|thumb|right|Hina ema (wooden plaques)]] '''Nagahama Shrine''' (長浜神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県|12=draft}}. The shrine's primary deity is {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=draft}}[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2015/12/28/132221197 新年の舞「めおと」で 雌の獅子頭奉納 大分市の長浜神社] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, December 28, 2015, and it also enshrines {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|12=draft}}[http://www.niigata-u.com/files/oita/nagahama.html Nagahama Shrine (Nagahama Town, Ōita City)] Ōita Shrine Exploration. Under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=draft}}, the shrine was classified as a gōsha. Nagahama Shrine is particularly revered by women for its association with the healing of gynecological ailments, as it enshrines deities regarded as patrons of medicine, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|12=draft}}[http://www.i-oita.net/spot/history_culture/oita/9182.html Nagahama Shrine] iNavi Ōita (Ōita Broadcasting System). Devotees traditionally offer wooden plaques (ema) depicting male and female hina dolls when making prayersKatsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 12: Around Nagahama" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita012a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, November 2, 2007. == History == According to shrine tradition, Nagahama Shrine was founded in {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永|12=draft}} 13 (1406). A local resident of Nagahama had a divine dream, and the following morning discovered a small wooden shrine box washed ashore, containing a miniature altar inscribed "Yoshu Nagahama Daimyōjin" on the inner side of its door and a sacred mirror inside. Upon reporting this discovery to {{ill|Ōtomo Chikayo|ja|大友親世|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Governor of Bungo Province|ja|豊後国守護|12=draft}}, Chikayo donated shrine land and funds for construction. The shrine was established and named "Nagahama Daimyōjin"Katsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 1: Around Shōwa Avenue" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, August 17, 2007. The shrine was damaged by the tsunami caused by the {{ill|Keichō Bungo earthquake|ja|慶長豊後地震|12=draft}} on {{ill|July 12 (lunar calendar)|ja|7月12日 (旧暦)|12=draft}} in {{ill|Bunroku|ja|文禄|12=draft}} 5 (1596 CE). It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)|1=Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)|12=draft}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町|12=draft}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和|12=draft}} 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝|12=draft}} 9 (1681 CE). In {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正|12=draft}} 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from {{ill|July 5|ja|7月5日|12=draft}} to {{ill|July 7|ja|7月7日|12=draft}}, is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿|12=draft}} processions, {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽|12=draft}}, and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政|12=draft}} 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the {{ill|Fuchū domain (Bungo)|ja|府内藩|12=draft}}, {{ill|Matsudaira Chikayoshi|ja|松平近説|12=draft}}, was halted in 1922 but resumed in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|12=draft}} 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of {{ill|Ligustrum japonicum|ja|ネズミモチ|lt=Privet|12=draft}} adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝|12=draft}} era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōita Station|ja|大分駅|12=draft}}, {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|九州旅客鉄道|12=draft}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ōita]] [[Category:History of Ōita]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines]] [[Category:completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]] [[ja:%E9%95%B7%E6%B5%9C%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E5%A4%A7%E5%88%86%E5%B8%82)]] [[ja:&action=edit]] [[ja:長浜神社]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)}}

The shrine was damaged by the tsunami caused by the {{ill|Keichō Bungo earthquake|ja|慶長豊後地震|12=draft|12=draft}} on {{ill|July 12 (lunar calendar)|ja|7月12日 (旧暦)|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Bunroku|ja|文禄|12=draft|12=draft}} 5 (1596 CE). It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)|1=Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町|12=draft|12=draft}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和|12=draft|12=draft}} 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝|12=draft|12=draft}} 9 (1681 CE). In {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正|12=draft|12=draft}} 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from {{ill|July 5|ja|7月5日|12=draft|12=draft}} to {{ill|July 7|ja|7月7日|12=draft|12=draft}}, is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft|12=draft}}. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿|12=draft|12=draft}} processions, {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽|12=draft|12=draft}}, and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政|12=draft|12=draft}} 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the {{ill|Fuchū domain (Bungo)|ja|府内藩|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Matsudaira Chikayoshi|ja|松平近説|12=draft|12=draft}}, was halted in 1922 but resumed in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|12=draft|12=draft}} 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of {{ill|Ligustrum japonicum|ja|ネズミモチ|lt=Privet|12=draft|12=draft}} adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝|12=draft|12=draft}} era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōita Station|ja|大分駅|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|九州旅客鉄道|12=draft|12=draft}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ōita]] [[Category:History of Ōita]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]] [[ja:%E9%95%B7%E6%B5%9C%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E5%A4%A7%E5%88%86%E5%B8%82)]] [[ja:&action=edit]] [[ja:長浜神社]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)}}

{{Shrine | name = Nagahama Shrine (長浜神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 2.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | location = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)|12=draft|12=draft}} 1-8-7 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=draft|12=draft}} | Shrine_rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|12=draft|12=draft}} | consecration_year = Tradition dates to {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永|12=draft|12=draft}} 13 (1406 CE) | Main_building = | Other_name = Nagahama-sama[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2016/06/21/135546234 「雨の長浜さま」あれこれ【おおいた祭時記】] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, June 21, 2016 | Events = Annual festival: July 5–7 | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Nagahama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 1.jpg|thumb|right|Torii gate]] [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 3.jpg|thumb|right|Hina ema (wooden plaques)]] '''Nagahama Shrine''' (長浜神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県|12=draft|12=draft}}. The shrine's primary deity is {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=draft|12=draft}}[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2015/12/28/132221197 新年の舞「めおと」で 雌の獅子頭奉納 大分市の長浜神社] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, December 28, 2015, and it also enshrines {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|12=draft|12=draft}}[http://www.niigata-u.com/files/oita/nagahama.html Nagahama Shrine (Nagahama Town, Ōita City)] Ōita Shrine Exploration. Under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=draft|12=draft}}, the shrine was classified as a gōsha. Nagahama Shrine is particularly revered by women for its association with the healing of gynecological ailments, as it enshrines deities regarded as patrons of medicine, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|12=draft|12=draft}}[http://www.i-oita.net/spot/history_culture/oita/9182.html Nagahama Shrine] iNavi Ōita (Ōita Broadcasting System). Devotees traditionally offer wooden plaques (ema) depicting male and female hina dolls when making prayersKatsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 12: Around Nagahama" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita012a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, November 2, 2007. == History == According to shrine tradition, Nagahama Shrine was founded in {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永|12=draft|12=draft}} 13 (1406). A local resident of Nagahama had a divine dream, and the following morning discovered a small wooden shrine box washed ashore, containing a miniature altar inscribed "Yoshu Nagahama Daimyōjin" on the inner side of its door and a sacred mirror inside. Upon reporting this discovery to {{ill|Ōtomo Chikayo|ja|大友親世|12=draft|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Governor of Bungo Province|ja|豊後国守護|12=draft|12=draft}}, Chikayo donated shrine land and funds for construction. The shrine was established and named "Nagahama Daimyōjin"Katsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 1: Around Shōwa Avenue" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, August 17, 2007. The shrine was damaged by the tsunami caused by the {{ill|Keichō Bungo earthquake|ja|慶長豊後地震|12=draft|12=draft}} on {{ill|July 12 (lunar calendar)|ja|7月12日 (旧暦)|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Bunroku|ja|文禄|12=draft|12=draft}} 5 (1596 CE). It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)|1=Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町|12=draft|12=draft}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和|12=draft|12=draft}} 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝|12=draft|12=draft}} 9 (1681 CE). In {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正|12=draft|12=draft}} 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from {{ill|July 5|ja|7月5日|12=draft|12=draft}} to {{ill|July 7|ja|7月7日|12=draft|12=draft}}, is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft|12=draft}}. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿|12=draft|12=draft}} processions, {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽|12=draft|12=draft}}, and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政|12=draft|12=draft}} 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the {{ill|Fuchū domain (Bungo)|ja|府内藩|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Matsudaira Chikayoshi|ja|松平近説|12=draft|12=draft}}, was halted in 1922 but resumed in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|12=draft|12=draft}} 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of {{ill|Ligustrum japonicum|ja|ネズミモチ|lt=Privet|12=draft|12=draft}} adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝|12=draft|12=draft}} era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōita Station|ja|大分駅|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|九州旅客鉄道|12=draft|12=draft}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ōita]] [[Category:History of Ōita]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]] [[ja:%E9%95%B7%E6%B5%9C%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E5%A4%A7%E5%88%86%E5%B8%82)]] [[ja:&action=edit]] [[ja:長浜神社]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)}}

Under the {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|12=draft|12=draft}}, the shrine was classified as a gōsha. Nagahama Shrine is particularly revered by women for its association with the healing of gynecological ailments, as it enshrines deities regarded as patrons of medicine, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|12=draft|12=draft}}[http://www.i-oita.net/spot/history_culture/oita/9182.html Nagahama Shrine] iNavi Ōita (Ōita Broadcasting System). Devotees traditionally offer wooden plaques (ema) depicting male and female hina dolls when making prayersKatsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 12: Around Nagahama" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita012a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, November 2, 2007. == History == According to shrine tradition, Nagahama Shrine was founded in {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永|12=draft|12=draft}} 13 (1406). A local resident of Nagahama had a divine dream, and the following morning discovered a small wooden shrine box washed ashore, containing a miniature altar inscribed "Yoshu Nagahama Daimyōjin" on the inner side of its door and a sacred mirror inside. Upon reporting this discovery to {{ill|Ōtomo Chikayo|ja|大友親世|12=draft|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Governor of Bungo Province|ja|豊後国守護|12=draft|12=draft}}, Chikayo donated shrine land and funds for construction. The shrine was established and named "Nagahama Daimyōjin"Katsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 1: Around Shōwa Avenue" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, August 17, 2007. The shrine was damaged by the tsunami caused by the {{ill|Keichō Bungo earthquake|ja|慶長豊後地震|12=draft|12=draft}} on {{ill|July 12 (lunar calendar)|ja|7月12日 (旧暦)|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Bunroku|ja|文禄|12=draft|12=draft}} 5 (1596 CE). It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)|1=Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町|12=draft|12=draft}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和|12=draft|12=draft}} 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝|12=draft|12=draft}} 9 (1681 CE). In {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正|12=draft|12=draft}} 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from {{ill|July 5|ja|7月5日|12=draft|12=draft}} to {{ill|July 7|ja|7月7日|12=draft|12=draft}}, is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft|12=draft}}. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿|12=draft|12=draft}} processions, {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽|12=draft|12=draft}}, and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政|12=draft|12=draft}} 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the {{ill|Fuchū domain (Bungo)|ja|府内藩|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Matsudaira Chikayoshi|ja|松平近説|12=draft|12=draft}}, was halted in 1922 but resumed in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|12=draft|12=draft}} 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of {{ill|Ligustrum japonicum|ja|ネズミモチ|lt=Privet|12=draft|12=draft}} adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝|12=draft|12=draft}} era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōita Station|ja|大分駅|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|九州旅客鉄道|12=draft|12=draft}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ōita]] [[Category:History of Ōita]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]] [[ja:%E9%95%B7%E6%B5%9C%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E5%A4%A7%E5%88%86%E5%B8%82)]] [[ja:&action=edit]] [[ja:長浜神社]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)}}

{{Shrine | name = Nagahama Shrine (長浜神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 2.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | location = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)}} 1-8-7 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona}} | Shrine_Rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | consecration_year = Tradition dates to {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永}} 13 (1406 CE) | Main_building = | Other_name = Nagahama-sama[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2016/06/21/135546234 「雨の長浜さま」あれこれ【おおいた祭時記】] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, June 21, 2016 | Events = Annual festival: July 5–7 | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Nagahama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 1.jpg|thumb|right|Torii gate]] [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 3.jpg|thumb|right|Hina ema (wooden plaques)]] '''Nagahama Shrine''' (長浜神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}. The shrine's primary deity is {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona}}[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2015/12/28/132221197 新年の舞「めおと」で 雌の獅子頭奉納 大分市の長浜神社] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, December 28, 2015, and it also enshrines {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane}}[http://www.niigata-u.com/files/oita/nagahama.html Nagahama Shrine (Nagahama Town, Ōita City)] Ōita Shrine Exploration. Under the {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines}}, the shrine was classified as a gōsha. Nagahama Shrine is particularly revered by women for its association with the healing of gynecological ailments, as it enshrines deities regarded as patrons of medicine, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}[http://www.i-oita.net/spot/history_culture/oita/9182.html Nagahama Shrine] iNavi Ōita (Ōita Broadcasting System). A local resident of Nagahama had a divine dream, and the following morning discovered a small wooden shrine box washed ashore, containing a miniature altar inscribed "Yoshu Nagahama Daimyōjin" on the inner side of its door and a sacred mirror inside. Upon reporting this discovery to {{ill|Ōtomo Chikayo|ja|大友親世}}, the {{ill|Governor of Bungo Province|ja|豊後国守護}}, Chikayo donated shrine land and funds for construction. The shrine was established and named "Nagahama Daimyōjin"Katsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 1: Around Shōwa Avenue" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, August 17, 2007. It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)|1=Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和}} 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝}} 9 (1681 CE). revision.comment: 13 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:長浜神社 (大分市) up to rev 102199076
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称 = 長浜神社 |画像 = [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 2.jpg|250px]]
拝殿 |所在地 = [[大分県]][[大分市]][[長浜町 (大分市)|長浜町]]一丁目8番7号 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = [[スクナビコナ|少彦名命]] |社格 = 旧[[郷社]] |創建 = 伝[[応永]]13年（[[1406年]]） |本殿 = |別名 = 長浜さま[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2016/06/21/135546234 「雨の長浜さま」あれこれ【おおいた祭時記】] 大分合同新聞、2016年6月21日 |札所等 = |例祭 = [[7月5日]] - [[7月7日|7日]] |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Oita|label=長浜神社|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 1.jpg|thumb|right|鳥居]] [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 3.jpg|thumb|right|雛絵馬]] '''長浜神社'''（ながはまじんじゃ）は、[[大分県]][[大分市]][[長浜町 (大分市)|長浜町]]一丁目8番7号に鎮座する[[神社]]である。主祭神は[[スクナビコナ|少彦名命]][https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2015/12/28/132221197 新年の舞「めおと」で 雌の獅子頭奉納 大分市の長浜神社] 大分合同新聞、2015年12月28日で、[[菅原道真]]を合祀する[http://www.niigata-u.com/files/oita/nagahama.html 長浜神社（大分市長浜町〈ながはままち〉）] おおいた神社探訪。[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は郷社。 医道の祖神である少彦名命及び大己貴命を祭神とすることから、[[婦人病]]平癒に霊験があるとされ、特に女性の信仰を集める[http://www.i-oita.net/spot/history_culture/oita/9182.html 長濱神社] iナビおおいた（大分放送）。祈願にあたっては、男女の立ち雛を描いたひな[[絵馬]]を奉納する習わしがある渡辺克己「{{PDFlink|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita012a1.pdf デジタルブック版「大分今昔」 第十二章 長浜かいわい]}}」 大分合同新聞社、2007年11月2日。 == 沿革 == 社伝によれば、創建は[[室町時代]]の[[応永]]13年（[[1406年]]）。ある晩、長浜の住人が夢でお告げを得て、その翌朝、海岸でお告げ通りに木箱に納められた小さな[[祠]]を見つけた。祠には、扉の裏に「豫州長濱大明神」と記され、中には[[神鏡]]が納められていた。この一件を[[豊後国]][[守護]]の[[大友親世]]に報告すると、親世は[[府内 (豊後国)|府内]]同慈寺東北地（現在の[[府内城|城址公園]]のあたり）の[[寺社領|社領]]と社殿の造営料を寄進し、神社を建立して長濱大明神と称した渡辺克己「{{PDFlink|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf デジタルブック版「大分今昔」 第一章 昭和通りかいわい]}}」 大分合同新聞社、2007年8月17日。 その後、[[文禄]]5年[[閏月|閏]][[7月12日 (旧暦)|7月12日]]（[[1596年]][[9月4日]]）に発生した[[慶長豊後地震]]この年には大地震が頻発したことから、文禄から[[慶長]]に改元された。この地震は改元後の慶長を付して慶長豊後地震と呼ばれるの[[津波]]によって被害を被り、一時、勢家町にある[[春日神社 (大分市)|春日神社]]の境内に遷された後、[[元和 (日本)|元和]]5年（[[1619年]]）に塩九升町に遷り、さらに[[延宝]]9年（[[1681年]]）に同じく塩九升町内の現在地に遷座した。[[大正]]2年（[[1913年]]）には、菅原道真を祭神とする隣接の松末天満社を合祀している。 == 夏季例大祭 == 毎年[[7月5日]]-[[7月7日|7日]]に行われる。通称、「長浜さま」。豊後三大市の一つに数えられた「長浜の市」が起源とされ、[[江戸時代]]に始まった。[[梅雨]]の最中に行われ、雨が降ることが多いために「雨の長浜さま」と呼ばれる。大分で最初に行われる夏祭りであり、大分に夏を告げる祭りとして親しまれている[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ 長浜神社夏祭り] 日本一の「おんせん県」大分県の観光情報公式サイト[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 長浜神社夏祭り] 一般社団法人 大分市観光協会[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c 大みこしを目玉ににぎわう きょうまで大分市・長浜神社／大分] 毎日新聞、2016年7月7日[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html 長濱神社夏季大祭] やっちょるでー!おおいた瓦版（大分銀行）。 祭りの間は[[歩行者天国]]となる塩九升通りや長浜通りに120を超える露店が並び、商店街には「風流見立て細工」が飾られる。[[神輿|みこし]]の巡行や[[神楽]]が行われ、初日の夜は、お祭り広場で[[YOSAKOI]]や[[フラダンス]]も行われる。みこし巡行は、[[安政]]3年（[[1856年]]）に[[府内藩]]最後の藩主[[松平近説]]が奉納したみこしが巡行したことに始まる。御神体をみこしに移して本殿を留守にするのは参拝者に申し訳ないとの理由で大正11年（[[1922年]]）に中止になったが、[[昭和]]62年（[[1987年]]）に再開されたものである。 また、祭りの期間中には、「おみか餅」という、[[ネズミモチ]]の枝に赤、黄、緑の[[餅]]や[[短冊]]を付けた[[縁起物]]が販売される。これは、「おみか」という女性が病気平癒の祈願をし、快癒のお礼に餅をつけた木を奉納したのが起源とされ、[[延宝]]年間（[[1673年]]-[[1681年]]）に作られるようになったという。戦時中一時中断したが、地元商店会を中心とする「おみか餅保存会」によって復活した。 == 交通 == * [[九州旅客鉄道|JR九州]][[大分駅]]から徒歩15分 == 脚注 == {{reflist|2}} == 外部リンク == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|長浜神社}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:なかはましんしや}} [[Category:大分県の神社]] [[Category:大分市の建築物]] [[Category:大分市の歴史]] [[Category:大分県の旧郷社]]

大分市の長浜神社] 大分合同新聞、2015年12月28日で、[[菅原道真]]を合祀する[http://www.niigata-u.com/files/oita/nagahama.html 長浜神社（大分市長浜町〈ながはままち〉）] おおいた神社探訪。[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は郷社。 医道の祖神である少彦名命及び大己貴命を祭神とすることから、[[婦人病]]平癒に霊験があるとされ、特に女性の信仰を集める[http://www.i-oita.net/spot/history_culture/oita/9182.html 長濱神社] iナビおおいた（大分放送）。祈願にあたっては、男女の立ち雛を描いたひな[[絵馬]]を奉納する習わしがある渡辺克己「{{PDFlink|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita012a1.pdf デジタルブック版「大分今昔」 第十二章 長浜かいわい]}}」 大分合同新聞社、2007年11月2日。 == 沿革 == 社伝によれば、創建は[[室町時代]]の[[応永]]13年（[[1406年]]）。ある晩、長浜の住人が夢でお告げを得て、その翌朝、海岸でお告げ通りに木箱に納められた小さな[[祠]]を見つけた。祠には、扉の裏に「豫州長濱大明神」と記され、中には[[神鏡]]が納められていた。この一件を[[豊後国]][[守護]]の[[大友親世]]に報告すると、親世は[[府内 (豊後国)|府内]]同慈寺東北地（現在の[[府内城|城址公園]]のあたり）の[[寺社領|社領]]と社殿の造営料を寄進し、神社を建立して長濱大明神と称した渡辺克己「{{PDFlink|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf デジタルブック版「大分今昔」 第一章 昭和通りかいわい]}}」 大分合同新聞社、2007年8月17日。 その後、[[文禄]]5年[[閏月|閏]][[7月12日 (旧暦)|7月12日]]（[[1596年]][[9月4日]]）に発生した[[慶長豊後地震]]この年には大地震が頻発したことから、文禄から[[慶長]]に改元された。この地震は改元後の慶長を付して慶長豊後地震と呼ばれるの[[津波]]によって被害を被り、一時、勢家町にある[[春日神社 (大分市)|春日神社]]の境内に遷された後、[[元和 (日本)|元和]]5年（[[1619年]]）に塩九升町に遷り、さらに[[延宝]]9年（[[1681年]]）に同じく塩九升町内の現在地に遷座した。[[大正]]2年（[[1913年]]）には、菅原道真を祭神とする隣接の松末天満社を合祀している。 == 夏季例大祭 == 毎年[[7月5日]]-[[7月7日|7日]]に行われる。通称、「長浜さま」。豊後三大市の一つに数えられた「長浜の市」が起源とされ、[[江戸時代]]に始まった。[[梅雨]]の最中に行われ、雨が降ることが多いために「雨の長浜さま」と呼ばれる。大分で最初に行われる夏祭りであり、大分に夏を告げる祭りとして親しまれている[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ 長浜神社夏祭り] 日本一の「おんせん県」大分県の観光情報公式サイト[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 長浜神社夏祭り] 一般社団法人 大分市観光協会[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c 大みこしを目玉ににぎわう きょうまで大分市・長浜神社／大分] 毎日新聞、2016年7月7日[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html 長濱神社夏季大祭] やっちょるでー!おおいた瓦版（大分銀行）。 祭りの間は[[歩行者天国]]となる塩九升通りや長浜通りに120を超える露店が並び、商店街には「風流見立て細工」が飾られる。[[神輿|みこし]]の巡行や[[神楽]]が行われ、初日の夜は、お祭り広場で[[YOSAKOI]]や[[フラダンス]]も行われる。みこし巡行は、[[安政]]3年（[[1856年]]）に[[府内藩]]最後の藩主[[松平近説]]が奉納したみこしが巡行したことに始まる。御神体をみこしに移して本殿を留守にするのは参拝者に申し訳ないとの理由で大正11年（[[1922年]]）に中止になったが、[[昭和]]62年（[[1987年]]）に再開されたものである。 また、祭りの期間中には、「おみか餅」という、[[ネズミモチ]]の枝に赤、黄、緑の[[餅]]や[[短冊]]を付けた[[縁起物]]が販売される。これは、「おみか」という女性が病気平癒の祈願をし、快癒のお礼に餅をつけた木を奉納したのが起源とされ、[[延宝]]年間（[[1673年]]-[[1681年]]）に作られるようになったという。戦時中一時中断したが、地元商店会を中心とする「おみか餅保存会」によって復活した。 == 交通 == * [[九州旅客鉄道|JR九州]][[大分駅]]から徒歩15分 == 脚注 == {{reflist|2}} == 外部リンク == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|長浜神社}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:なかはましんしや}} [[Category:大分県の神社]] [[Category:大分市の建築物]] [[Category:大分市の歴史]] [[Category:大分県の旧郷社]]

{{Shrine | name = Nagahama Shrine (長浜神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 2.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | location = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)|12=draft}} 1-8-7 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=draft}} | Shrine_Rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|12=draft}} | consecration_year = Tradition dates to {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永|12=draft}} 13 (1406 CE) | Main_building = | Other_name = Nagahama-sama[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2016/06/21/135546234 「雨の長浜さま」あれこれ【おおいた祭時記】] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, June 21, 2016 | Events = Annual festival: July 5–7 | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Nagahama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 1.jpg|thumb|right|Torii gate]] [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 3.jpg|thumb|right|Hina ema (wooden plaques)]] '''Nagahama Shrine''' (長浜神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県|12=draft}}. The shrine's primary deity is {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=draft}}[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2015/12/28/132221197 新年の舞「めおと」で 雌の獅子頭奉納 大分市の長浜神社] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, December 28, 2015, and it also enshrines {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|12=draft}}[http://www.niigata-u.com/files/oita/nagahama.html Nagahama Shrine (Nagahama Town, Ōita City)] Ōita Shrine Exploration. Under the {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|12=draft}}, the shrine was classified as a gōsha. Nagahama Shrine is particularly revered by women for its association with the healing of gynecological ailments, as it enshrines deities regarded as patrons of medicine, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|12=draft}}[http://www.i-oita.net/spot/history_culture/oita/9182.html Nagahama Shrine] iNavi Ōita (Ōita Broadcasting System). Devotees traditionally offer wooden plaques (ema) depicting male and female hina dolls when making prayersKatsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 12: Around Nagahama" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita012a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, November 2, 2007. == History == According to shrine tradition, Nagahama Shrine was founded in {{ill|Ōei|ja|応永|12=draft}} 13 (1406). A local resident of Nagahama had a divine dream, and the following morning discovered a small wooden shrine box washed ashore, containing a miniature altar inscribed "Yoshu Nagahama Daimyōjin" on the inner side of its door and a sacred mirror inside. Upon reporting this discovery to {{ill|Ōtomo Chikayo|ja|大友親世|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Governor of Bungo Province|ja|豊後国守護|12=draft}}, Chikayo donated shrine land and funds for construction. The shrine was established and named "Nagahama Daimyōjin"Katsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 1: Around Shōwa Avenue" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, August 17, 2007. The shrine was damaged by the tsunami caused by the {{ill|Keichō Bungo earthquake|ja|慶長豊後地震|12=draft}} on {{ill|July 12 (lunar calendar)|ja|7月12日 (旧暦)|12=draft}} in {{ill|Bunroku|ja|文禄|12=draft}} 5 (1596 CE). It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)|1=Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)|12=draft}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町|12=draft}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in {{ill|Genna|ja|元和|12=draft}} 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝|12=draft}} 9 (1681 CE). In {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正|12=draft}} 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from {{ill|July 5|ja|7月5日|12=draft}} to {{ill|July 7|ja|7月7日|12=draft}}, is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿|12=draft}} processions, {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽|12=draft}}, and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政|12=draft}} 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the {{ill|Fuchū domain (Bungo)|ja|府内藩|12=draft}}, {{ill|Matsudaira Chikayoshi|ja|松平近説|12=draft}}, was halted in 1922 but resumed in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|12=draft}} 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of {{ill|Ligustrum japonicum|ja|ネズミモチ|lt=Privet|12=draft}} adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the {{ill|Empō|ja|延宝|12=draft}} era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōita Station|ja|大分駅|12=draft}}, {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|九州旅客鉄道|12=draft}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} {{translated page|ja|長浜神社 (大分市)|version=102199076|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ōita]] [[Category:History of Ōita]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]] [[ja:%E9%95%B7%E6%B5%9C%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E5%A4%A7%E5%88%86%E5%B8%82)]] [[ja:&action=edit]] [[ja:長浜神社]]
