Upon reporting this discovery to {{ill|Ōtomo Chikayo|ja|大友親世|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Chikayo|qq=}}, the {{ill|Governor of Bungo Province|ja|豊後国守護|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor of Bungo Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Chikayo donated shrine land and funds for construction. The shrine was established and named "Nagahama Daimyōjin"Katsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 1: Around Shōwa Avenue" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, August 17, 2007. The shrine was damaged by the tsunami caused by the {{ill|Keichō Bungo earthquake|ja|慶長豊後地震|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keichō Bungo earthquake|qq=}} on {{ill|July 12 (Lunisolar)|lt=July 12 (Lunisolar)|lt=July 12 (lunar calendar)|ja|7月12日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 12 (lunar calendar)|qq=}} in [[:en:Bunroku|Bunroku]] 5 (1596 CE). It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)|1=Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)|qq=}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seie Town (Ōita)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in [[:en:Yuanhe|Genna]] 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in [[:en:Enpō|Empō]] 9 (1681 CE). In [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō]] 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from [[:en:July 5|July 5]] to [[:en:July 7|July 7]], is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include [[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]] processions, [[:en:Kagura|Kagura]], and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to [[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the [[:en:Funai Domain|Fuchū domain (Bungo)]], [[:en:Matsudaira Chikayoshi|Matsudaira Chikayoshi]], was halted in 1922 but resumed in [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of [[:en:Ligustrum japonicum|Privet]] adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the [[:en:Enpō|Empō]] era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from [[:en:Ōita Station|Ōita Station]], [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|JR Kyushu]] == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} {{translated page|ja|長浜神社 (大分市)|version=102199076|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Gō-sha]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]] [[sn:E9%95%B7%E6%B5%9C%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E5%A4%A7%E5%88%86%E5%B8%82)]] [[ja:長浜神社]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Ōita Prefecture]] [[ja:長浜神社 (大分市)]] [[Category:Pages with no Wikidata]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11653315]] Wikidata name: Nagahama-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:22, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Shrine | name = Nagahama Shrine (長浜神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 2.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | location = [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]], [[:en:Ōita (city)|Ōita City]], {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|qq=}} 1-8-7 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} | Shrine_Rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#District_shrine|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} | consecration_year = Tradition dates to [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]] 13 (1406 CE) | Main_building = | Other_name = Nagahama-sama[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2016/06/21/135546234 「雨の長浜さま」あれこれ【おおいた祭時記】] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, June 21, 2016 | Events = Annual festival: July 5–7 | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Nagahama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 1.jpg|thumb|right|Torii gate]] [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 3.jpg|thumb|right|Hina ema (wooden plaques)]] '''Nagahama Shrine''' (長浜神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|qq=}}, [[:en:Ōita (city)|Ōita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]]. The shrine's primary deity is {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2015/12/28/132221197 新年の舞「めおと」で 雌の獅子頭奉納 大分市の長浜神社] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, December 28, 2015, and it also enshrines {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}[http://www.niigata-u.com/files/oita/nagahama.html Nagahama Shrine (Nagahama Town, Ōita City)] Ōita Shrine Exploration. Under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], the shrine was classified as a gōsha. Nagahama Shrine is particularly revered by women for its association with the healing of gynecological ailments, as it enshrines deities regarded as patrons of medicine, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}[http://www.i-oita.net/spot/history_culture/oita/9182.html Nagahama Shrine] iNavi Ōita (Ōita Broadcasting System). Devotees traditionally offer wooden plaques (ema) depicting male and female hina dolls when making prayersKatsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 12: Around Nagahama" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita012a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, November 2, 2007. == History == According to shrine tradition, Nagahama Shrine was founded in [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]] 13 (1406). A local resident of Nagahama had a divine dream, and the following morning discovered a small wooden shrine box washed ashore, containing a miniature altar inscribed "Yoshu Nagahama Daimyōjin" on the inner side of its door and a sacred mirror inside. Upon reporting this discovery to {{ill|Ōtomo Chikayo|ja|大友親世|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Chikayo|qq=}}, the {{ill|Governor of Bungo Province|ja|豊後国守護|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor of Bungo Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Chikayo donated shrine land and funds for construction. The shrine was established and named "Nagahama Daimyōjin"Katsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 1: Around Shōwa Avenue" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, August 17, 2007. The shrine was damaged by the tsunami caused by the {{ill|Keichō Bungo earthquake|ja|慶長豊後地震|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keichō Bungo earthquake|qq=}} on {{ill|July 12 (Lunisolar)|lt=July 12 (Lunisolar)|lt=July 12 (lunar calendar)|ja|7月12日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 12 (lunar calendar)|qq=}} in [[:en:Bunroku|Bunroku]] 5 (1596 CE). It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)|1=Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)|qq=}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seie Town (Ōita)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in [[:en:Yuanhe|Genna]] 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in [[:en:Enpō|Empō]] 9 (1681 CE). In [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō]] 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from [[:en:July 5|July 5]] to [[:en:July 7|July 7]], is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include [[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]] processions, [[:en:Kagura|Kagura]], and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to [[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the [[:en:Funai Domain|Fuchū domain (Bungo)]], [[:en:Matsudaira Chikayoshi|Matsudaira Chikayoshi]], was halted in 1922 but resumed in [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of [[:en:Ligustrum japonicum|Privet]] adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the [[:en:Enpō|Empō]] era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from [[:en:Ōita Station|Ōita Station]], [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|JR Kyushu]] == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} {{translated page|ja|長浜神社 (大分市)|version=102199076|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Gō-sha]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]] [[sn:E9%95%B7%E6%B5%9C%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E5%A4%A7%E5%88%86%E5%B8%82)]] [[ja:長浜神社]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Ōita Prefecture]] [[ja:長浜神社 (大分市)]] [[Category:Pages with no Wikidata]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11653315]] Wikidata name: Nagahama-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:22, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nagahama Shrine (長浜神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 2.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | location = [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]], [[:en:Ōita (city)|Ōita City]], {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|qq=}} 1-8-7 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} | Shrine_Rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#District_shrine|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} | consecration_year = Tradition dates to [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]] 13 (1406 CE) | Main_building = | Other_name = Nagahama-sama[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2016/06/21/135546234 「雨の長浜さま」あれこれ【おおいた祭時記】] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, June 21, 2016 | Events = Annual festival: July 5–7 | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Nagahama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 1.jpg|thumb|right|Torii gate]] [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 3.jpg|thumb|right|Hina ema (wooden plaques)]] '''Nagahama Shrine''' (長浜神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|qq=}}, [[:en:Ōita (city)|Ōita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]]. The shrine's primary deity is {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2015/12/28/132221197 新年の舞「めおと」で 雌の獅子頭奉納 大分市の長浜神社] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, December 28, 2015, and it also enshrines {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}[http://www.niigata-u.com/files/oita/nagahama.html Nagahama Shrine (Nagahama Town, Ōita City)] Ōita Shrine Exploration. Under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], the shrine was classified as a gōsha. Nagahama Shrine is particularly revered by women for its association with the healing of gynecological ailments, as it enshrines deities regarded as patrons of medicine, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}[http://www.i-oita.net/spot/history_culture/oita/9182.html Nagahama Shrine] iNavi Ōita (Ōita Broadcasting System). Devotees traditionally offer wooden plaques (ema) depicting male and female hina dolls when making prayersKatsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 12: Around Nagahama" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita012a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, November 2, 2007. == History == According to shrine tradition, Nagahama Shrine was founded in [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]] 13 (1406). A local resident of Nagahama had a divine dream, and the following morning discovered a small wooden shrine box washed ashore, containing a miniature altar inscribed "Yoshu Nagahama Daimyōjin" on the inner side of its door and a sacred mirror inside. Upon reporting this discovery to {{ill|Ōtomo Chikayo|ja|大友親世|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Chikayo|qq=}}, the {{ill|Governor of Bungo Province|ja|豊後国守護|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor of Bungo Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Chikayo donated shrine land and funds for construction. The shrine was established and named "Nagahama Daimyōjin"Katsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 1: Around Shōwa Avenue" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, August 17, 2007. The shrine was damaged by the tsunami caused by the {{ill|Keichō Bungo earthquake|ja|慶長豊後地震|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keichō Bungo earthquake|qq=}} on {{ill|July 12 (Lunisolar)|lt=July 12 (Lunisolar)|lt=July 12 (lunar calendar)|ja|7月12日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 12 (lunar calendar)|qq=}} in [[:en:Bunroku|Bunroku]] 5 (1596 CE). It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)|1=Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)|qq=}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seie Town (Ōita)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in [[:en:Yuanhe|Genna]] 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in [[:en:Enpō|Empō]] 9 (1681 CE). In [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō]] 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from [[:en:July 5|July 5]] to [[:en:July 7|July 7]], is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include [[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]] processions, [[:en:Kagura|Kagura]], and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to [[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the [[:en:Funai Domain|Fuchū domain (Bungo)]], [[:en:Matsudaira Chikayoshi|Matsudaira Chikayoshi]], was halted in 1922 but resumed in [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of [[:en:Ligustrum japonicum|Privet]] adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the [[:en:Enpō|Empō]] era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from [[:en:Ōita Station|Ōita Station]], [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|JR Kyushu]] == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} {{translated page|ja|長浜神社 (大分市)|version=102199076|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Gō-sha]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]] [[sn:E9%95%B7%E6%B5%9C%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E5%A4%A7%E5%88%86%E5%B8%82)]] [[ja:長浜神社]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Ōita Prefecture]] [[ja:長浜神社 (大分市)]]

Under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], the shrine was classified as a gōsha. Nagahama Shrine is particularly revered by women for its association with the healing of gynecological ailments, as it enshrines deities regarded as patrons of medicine, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}[http://www.i-oita.net/spot/history_culture/oita/9182.html Nagahama Shrine] iNavi Ōita (Ōita Broadcasting System). Devotees traditionally offer wooden plaques (ema) depicting male and female hina dolls when making prayersKatsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 12: Around Nagahama" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita012a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, November 2, 2007. == History == According to shrine tradition, Nagahama Shrine was founded in [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]] 13 (1406). A local resident of Nagahama had a divine dream, and the following morning discovered a small wooden shrine box washed ashore, containing a miniature altar inscribed "Yoshu Nagahama Daimyōjin" on the inner side of its door and a sacred mirror inside. Upon reporting this discovery to {{ill|Ōtomo Chikayo|ja|大友親世|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Chikayo|qq=|1=Ōtomo Chikayo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433259}}, the {{ill|Governor of Bungo Province|ja|豊後国守護|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor of Bungo Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Chikayo donated shrine land and funds for construction. The shrine was established and named "Nagahama Daimyōjin"Katsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 1: Around Shōwa Avenue" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, August 17, 2007. The shrine was damaged by the tsunami caused by the {{ill|Keichō Bungo earthquake|ja|慶長豊後地震|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keichō Bungo earthquake|qq=|1=1596 Bungo earthquake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495196}} on {{ill|July 12 (Lunisolar)|lt=July 12 (Lunisolar)|lt=July 12 (lunar calendar)|ja|7月12日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 12 (lunar calendar)|qq=}} in [[:en:Bunroku|Bunroku]] 5 (1596 CE). It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)|1=Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)|qq=}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seie Town (Ōita)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in [[:en:Yuanhe|Genna]] 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in [[:en:Enpō|Empō]] 9 (1681 CE). In [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō]] 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from [[:en:July 5|July 5]] to [[:en:July 7|July 7]], is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include [[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]] processions, [[:en:Kagura|Kagura]], and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to [[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the [[:en:Funai Domain|Fuchū domain (Bungo)]], [[:en:Matsudaira Chikayoshi|Matsudaira Chikayoshi]], was halted in 1922 but resumed in [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of [[:en:Ligustrum japonicum|Privet]] adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the [[:en:Enpō|Empō]] era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from [[:en:Ōita Station|Ōita Station]], [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|JR Kyushu]] == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} {{translated page|ja|長浜神社 (大分市)|version=102199076|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Gō-sha]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]] [[sn:E9%95%B7%E6%B5%9C%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E5%A4%A7%E5%88%86%E5%B8%82)]] [[ja:長浜神社]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Ōita Prefecture]] [[ja:長浜神社 (大分市)]]

Under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], the shrine was classified as a gōsha. It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include [[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]] processions, [[:en:Kagura|Kagura]], and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to [[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the [[:en:Funai Domain|Fuchū domain (Bungo)]], [[:en:Matsudaira Chikayoshi|Matsudaira Chikayoshi]], was halted in 1922 but resumed in [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of [[:en:Ligustrum japonicum|Privet]] adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the [[:en:Enpō|Empō]] era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from [[:en:Ōita Station|Ōita Station]], [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|JR Kyushu]] == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} {{translated page|ja|長浜神社 (大分市)|version=102199076|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Gō-sha]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]] [[sn:E9%95%B7%E6%B5%9C%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E5%A4%A7%E5%88%86%E5%B8%82)]] [[ja:長浜神社]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Ōita Prefecture]] [[ja:長浜神社 (大分市)]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11653315]] Wikidata name: Nagahama-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:22, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nagahama Shrine (長浜神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 2.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | location = [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]], [[:en:Ōita (city)|Ōita City]], {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|qq=|1=Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48760693}} 1-8-7 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} | Shrine_Rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|qid=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | consecration_year = Tradition dates to [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]] 13 (1406 CE) | Main_building = | Other_name = Nagahama-sama[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2016/06/21/135546234 「雨の長浜さま」あれこれ【おおいた祭時記】] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, June 21, 2016 | Events = Annual festival: July 5–7 | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Nagahama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 1.jpg|thumb|right|Torii gate]] [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 3.jpg|thumb|right|Hina ema (wooden plaques)]] '''Nagahama Shrine''' (長浜神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|qq=|1=Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48760693}}, [[:en:Ōita (city)|Ōita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]]. The shrine's primary deity is {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2015/12/28/132221197 新年の舞「めおと」で 雌の獅子頭奉納 大分市の長浜神社] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, December 28, 2015, and it also enshrines {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}}[http://www.niigata-u.com/files/oita/nagahama.html Nagahama Shrine (Nagahama Town, Ōita City)] Ōita Shrine Exploration. Under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], the shrine was classified as a gōsha. Nagahama Shrine is particularly revered by women for its association with the healing of gynecological ailments, as it enshrines deities regarded as patrons of medicine, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}[http://www.i-oita.net/spot/history_culture/oita/9182.html Nagahama Shrine] iNavi Ōita (Ōita Broadcasting System). Devotees traditionally offer wooden plaques (ema) depicting male and female hina dolls when making prayersKatsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 12: Around Nagahama" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita012a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, November 2, 2007. == History == According to shrine tradition, Nagahama Shrine was founded in [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]] 13 (1406). A local resident of Nagahama had a divine dream, and the following morning discovered a small wooden shrine box washed ashore, containing a miniature altar inscribed "Yoshu Nagahama Daimyōjin" on the inner side of its door and a sacred mirror inside. Upon reporting this discovery to {{ill|Ōtomo Chikayo|ja|大友親世|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Chikayo|qq=|1=Ōtomo Chikayo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433259}}, the {{ill|Governor of Bungo Province|ja|豊後国守護|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor of Bungo Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Chikayo donated shrine land and funds for construction. The shrine was established and named "Nagahama Daimyōjin"Katsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 1: Around Shōwa Avenue" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, August 17, 2007. The shrine was damaged by the tsunami caused by the {{ill|Keichō Bungo earthquake|ja|慶長豊後地震|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keichō Bungo earthquake|qq=|1=1596 Bungo earthquake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495196}} on {{ill|July 12 (Lunisolar)|lt=July 12 (Lunisolar)|lt=July 12 (lunar calendar)|ja|7月12日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 12 (lunar calendar)|qq=}} in [[:en:Bunroku|Bunroku]] 5 (1596 CE). It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)|1=Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)|qq=}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seie Town (Ōita)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in [[:en:Yuanhe|Genna]] 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in [[:en:Enpō|Empō]] 9 (1681 CE). In [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō]] 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from [[:en:July 5|July 5]] to [[:en:July 7|July 7]], is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include [[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]] processions, [[:en:Kagura|Kagura]], and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to [[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the [[:en:Funai Domain|Fuchū domain (Bungo)]], [[:en:Matsudaira Chikayoshi|Matsudaira Chikayoshi]], was halted in 1922 but resumed in [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of [[:en:Ligustrum japonicum|Privet]] adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the [[:en:Enpō|Empō]] era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from [[:en:Ōita Station|Ōita Station]], [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|JR Kyushu]] == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} {{translated page|ja|長浜神社 (大分市)|version=102199076|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Gō-sha]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[sn:Nagahama Shrine (Ōita)]] [[sn:E9%95%B7%E6%B5%9C%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E5%A4%A7%E5%88%86%E5%B8%82)]] [[ja:長浜神社]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Ōita Prefecture]] [[ja:長浜神社 (大分市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11653315}}

The tradition dates to the [[:en:Enpō|Empō]] era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from [[:en:Ōita Station|Ōita Station]], [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|JR Kyushu]] == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} {{translated page|ja|長浜神社 (大分市)|version=102199076|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Gō-sha]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[ja:長浜神社]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Ōita Prefecture]] [[ja:長浜神社 (大分市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11653315}}

Haiden (Worship Hall) | location = [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]], [[:en:Ōita (city)|Ōita City]], {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|qq=|1=Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48760693}} 1-8-7 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} | Shrine_Rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|qid=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | consecration_year = Tradition dates to [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]] 13 (1406 CE) | Main_building = | Other_name = Nagahama-sama[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2016/06/21/135546234 「雨の長浜さま」あれこれ【おおいた祭時記】] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, June 21, 2016 | Events = Annual festival: July 5–7 | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Nagahama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 1.jpg|thumb|right|Torii gate]] [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 3.jpg|thumb|right|Hina ema (wooden plaques)]] '''Nagahama Shrine''' (長浜神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|qq=|1=Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48760693}}, [[:en:Ōita (city)|Ōita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]]. The shrine's primary deity is {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2015/12/28/132221197 新年の舞「めおと」で 雌の獅子頭奉納 大分市の長浜神社] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, December 28, 2015, and it also enshrines {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}}[http://www.niigata-u.com/files/oita/nagahama.html Nagahama Shrine (Nagahama Town, Ōita City)] Ōita Shrine Exploration. Under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], the shrine was classified as a gōsha. Nagahama Shrine is particularly revered by women for its association with the healing of gynecological ailments, as it enshrines deities regarded as patrons of medicine, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}[http://www.i-oita.net/spot/history_culture/oita/9182.html Nagahama Shrine] iNavi Ōita (Ōita Broadcasting System). Devotees traditionally offer wooden plaques (ema) depicting male and female hina dolls when making prayersKatsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 12: Around Nagahama" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita012a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, November 2, 2007. == History == According to shrine tradition, Nagahama Shrine was founded in [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]] 13 (1406). A local resident of Nagahama had a divine dream, and the following morning discovered a small wooden shrine box washed ashore, containing a miniature altar inscribed "Yoshu Nagahama Daimyōjin" on the inner side of its door and a sacred mirror inside. Upon reporting this discovery to {{ill|Ōtomo Chikayo|ja|大友親世|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Chikayo|qq=|1=Ōtomo Chikayo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433259}}, the {{ill|Governor of Bungo Province|ja|豊後国守護|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor of Bungo Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Chikayo donated shrine land and funds for construction. The shrine was established and named "Nagahama Daimyōjin"Katsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 1: Around Shōwa Avenue" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, August 17, 2007. The shrine was damaged by the tsunami caused by the {{ill|Keichō Bungo earthquake|ja|慶長豊後地震|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keichō Bungo earthquake|qq=|1=1596 Bungo earthquake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495196}} on {{ill|July 12 (Lunisolar)|lt=July 12 (Lunisolar)|lt=July 12 (lunar calendar)|ja|7月12日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 12 (lunar calendar)|qq=}} in [[:en:Bunroku|Bunroku]] 5 (1596 CE). It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)|1=Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga Shrine (Ōita City)|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)|qq=}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seie Town (Ōita)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in [[:en:Yuanhe|Genna]] 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in [[:en:Enpō|Empō]] 9 (1681 CE). In [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō]] 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from [[:en:July 5|July 5]] to [[:en:July 7|July 7]], is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include [[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]] processions, [[:en:Kagura|Kagura]], and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to [[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the [[:en:Funai Domain|Fuchū domain (Bungo)]], [[:en:Matsudaira Chikayoshi|Matsudaira Chikayoshi]], was halted in 1922 but resumed in [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of [[:en:Ligustrum japonicum|Privet]] adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the [[:en:Enpō|Empō]] era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from [[:en:Ōita Station|Ōita Station]], [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|JR Kyushu]] == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} {{translated page|ja|長浜神社 (大分市)|version=102199076|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Gō-sha]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[ja:長浜神社]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Ōita Prefecture]] [[ja:長浜神社 (大分市)]] {{wikidata link|Q28682890}}

Nagahama Shrine is particularly revered by women for its association with the healing of gynecological ailments, as it enshrines deities regarded as patrons of medicine, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}[http://www.i-oita.net/spot/history_culture/oita/9182.html Nagahama Shrine] iNavi Ōita (Ōita Broadcasting System). Haiden (Worship Hall) | location = [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]], [[:en:Ōita (city)|Ōita City]], {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|qq=|1=Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48760693}} 1-8-7 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} | Shrine_Rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|qid=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | consecration_year = Tradition dates to [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]] 13 (1406 CE) | Main_building = | Other_name = Nagahama-sama[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2016/06/21/135546234 「雨の長浜さま」あれこれ【おおいた祭時記】] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, June 21, 2016 | Events = Annual festival: July 5–7 | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Nagahama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 1.jpg|thumb|right|Torii gate]] [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 3.jpg|thumb|right|Hina ema (wooden plaques)]] '''Nagahama Shrine''' (長浜神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|qq=|1=Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48760693}}, [[:en:Ōita (city)|Ōita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]]. The shrine's primary deity is {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2015/12/28/132221197 新年の舞「めおと」で 雌の獅子頭奉納 大分市の長浜神社] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, December 28, 2015, and it also enshrines {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}}[http://www.niigata-u.com/files/oita/nagahama.html Nagahama Shrine (Nagahama Town, Ōita City)] Ōita Shrine Exploration. Under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], the shrine was classified as a gōsha. Nagahama Shrine is particularly revered by women for its association with the healing of gynecological ailments, as it enshrines deities regarded as patrons of medicine, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}[http://www.i-oita.net/spot/history_culture/oita/9182.html Nagahama Shrine] iNavi Ōita (Ōita Broadcasting System). Devotees traditionally offer wooden plaques (ema) depicting male and female hina dolls when making prayersKatsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 12: Around Nagahama" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita012a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, November 2, 2007. == History == According to shrine tradition, Nagahama Shrine was founded in [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]] 13 (1406). A local resident of Nagahama had a divine dream, and the following morning discovered a small wooden shrine box washed ashore, containing a miniature altar inscribed "Yoshu Nagahama Daimyōjin" on the inner side of its door and a sacred mirror inside. Upon reporting this discovery to {{ill|Ōtomo Chikayo|ja|大友親世|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Chikayo|qq=|1=Ōtomo Chikayo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433259}}, the {{ill|Governor of Bungo Province|ja|豊後国守護|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor of Bungo Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Chikayo donated shrine land and funds for construction. The shrine was established and named "Nagahama Daimyōjin"Katsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 1: Around Shōwa Avenue" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, August 17, 2007. The shrine was damaged by the tsunami caused by the {{ill|Keichō Bungo earthquake|ja|慶長豊後地震|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keichō Bungo earthquake|qq=|1=1596 Bungo earthquake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495196}} on {{ill|July 12 (Lunisolar)|lt=July 12|ja|7月12日 (旧暦)|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 12 (lunar calendar)}} in [[:en:Bunroku|Bunroku]] 5 (1596 CE). It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)|lt=Kasuga Shrine}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seie Town (Ōita)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|lt=Seie Town}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in [[:en:Yuanhe|Genna]] 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in [[:en:Enpō|Empō]] 9 (1681 CE). In [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō]] 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from [[:en:July 5|July 5]] to [[:en:July 7|July 7]], is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include [[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]] processions, [[:en:Kagura|Kagura]], and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to [[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the [[:en:Funai Domain|Fuchū domain (Bungo)]], [[:en:Matsudaira Chikayoshi|Matsudaira Chikayoshi]], was halted in 1922 but resumed in [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of [[:en:Ligustrum japonicum|Privet]] adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the [[:en:Enpō|Empō]] era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from [[:en:Ōita Station|Ōita Station]], [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|JR Kyushu]] == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} {{translated page|ja|長浜神社 (大分市)|version=102199076|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Gō-sha]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[ja:長浜神社]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Ōita Prefecture]] [[ja:長浜神社 (大分市)]] {{wikidata link|Q28682890}}

Devotees traditionally offer wooden plaques (ema) depicting male and female hina dolls when making prayersKatsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 12: Around Nagahama" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita012a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, November 2, 2007. == History == According to shrine tradition, Nagahama Shrine was founded in [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]] 13 (1406). A local resident of Nagahama had a divine dream, and the following morning discovered a small wooden shrine box washed ashore, containing a miniature altar inscribed "Yoshu Nagahama Daimyōjin" on the inner side of its door and a sacred mirror inside. Upon reporting this discovery to {{ill|Ōtomo Chikayo|ja|大友親世|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Chikayo|qq=|1=Ōtomo Chikayo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433259}}, the {{ill|Governor of Bungo Province|ja|豊後国守護|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor of Bungo Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Chikayo donated shrine land and funds for construction. The shrine was established and named "Nagahama Daimyōjin"Katsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 1: Around Shōwa Avenue" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, August 17, 2007. The shrine was damaged by the tsunami caused by the {{ill|Keichō Bungo earthquake|ja|慶長豊後地震|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keichō Bungo earthquake|qq=|1=1596 Bungo earthquake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495196}} on {{ill|July 12 (Lunisolar)|lt=July 12|ja|7月12日 (旧暦)|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 12 (lunar calendar)}} in [[:en:Bunroku|Bunroku]] 5 (1596 CE). It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)|lt=Kasuga Shrine}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seie Town (Ōita)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|lt=Seie Town}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in [[:en:Yuanhe|Genna]] 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in [[:en:Enpō|Empō]] 9 (1681 CE). In [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō]] 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from [[:en:July 5|July 5]] to [[:en:July 7|July 7]], is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include [[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]] processions, [[:en:Kagura|Kagura]], and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to [[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the [[:en:Funai Domain|Fuchū domain (Bungo)]], [[:en:Matsudaira Chikayoshi|Matsudaira Chikayoshi]], was halted in 1922 but resumed in [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of [[:en:Ligustrum japonicum|Privet]] adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the [[:en:Enpō|Empō]] era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from [[:en:Ōita Station|Ōita Station]], [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|JR Kyushu]] == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} {{translated page|ja|長浜神社 (大分市)|version=102199076|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Gō-sha]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[ja:長浜神社]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Ōita Prefecture]] [[ja:長浜神社 (大分市)]] {{wikidata link|Q28682890}}

The shrine's primary deity is {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2015/12/28/132221197 新年の舞「めおと」で 雌の獅子頭奉納 大分市の長浜神社] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, December 28, 2015, and it also enshrines {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}}[http://www.niigata-u.com/files/oita/nagahama.html Nagahama Shrine (Nagahama Town, Ōita City)] Ōita Shrine Exploration. Under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], the shrine was classified as a gōsha. Nagahama Shrine is particularly revered by women for its association with the healing of gynecological ailments, as it enshrines deities regarded as patrons of medicine, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}[http://www.i-oita.net/spot/history_culture/oita/9182.html Nagahama Shrine] iNavi Ōita (Ōita Broadcasting System). Devotees traditionally offer wooden plaques (ema) depicting male and female hina dolls when making prayersKatsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 12: Around Nagahama" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita012a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, November 2, 2007. == History == According to shrine tradition, Nagahama Shrine was founded in [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]] 13 (1406). revision.text:
[[ja:長浜神社 (大分市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11653315]] Wikidata name: Nagahama-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:22, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nagahama Shrine (長浜神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 2.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | location = [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]], [[:en:Ōita (city)|Ōita City]], {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|1=Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48760693}} 1-8-7 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} | Shrine_Rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | consecration_year = Tradition dates to [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]] 13 (1406 CE) | Main_building = | Other_name = Nagahama-sama[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2016/06/21/135546234 「雨の長浜さま」あれこれ【おおいた祭時記】] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, June 21, 2016 | Events = Annual festival: July 5–7 | Map = {{location map|Japan Oita|label=Nagahama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 1.jpg|thumb|right|Torii gate]] [[File:Oita nagahama jinja 3.jpg|thumb|right|Hina ema (wooden plaques)]] '''Nagahama Shrine''' (長浜神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|ja|長浜町 (大分市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|1=Nagahama Town (Ōita City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48760693}}, [[:en:Ōita (city)|Ōita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]]. The shrine's primary deity is {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}[https://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2015/12/28/132221197 新年の舞「めおと」で 雌の獅子頭奉納 大分市の長浜神社] Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, December 28, 2015, and it also enshrines {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}}[http://www.niigata-u.com/files/oita/nagahama.html Nagahama Shrine (Nagahama Town, Ōita City)] Ōita Shrine Exploration. Under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], the shrine was classified as a gōsha. Nagahama Shrine is particularly revered by women for its association with the healing of gynecological ailments, as it enshrines deities regarded as patrons of medicine, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}[http://www.i-oita.net/spot/history_culture/oita/9182.html Nagahama Shrine] iNavi Ōita (Ōita Broadcasting System). Devotees traditionally offer wooden plaques (ema) depicting male and female hina dolls when making prayersKatsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 12: Around Nagahama" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita012a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, November 2, 2007. == History == According to shrine tradition, Nagahama Shrine was founded in [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]] 13 (1406). A local resident of Nagahama had a divine dream, and the following morning discovered a small wooden shrine box washed ashore, containing a miniature altar inscribed "Yoshu Nagahama Daimyōjin" on the inner side of its door and a sacred mirror inside. Upon reporting this discovery to {{ill|Ōtomo Chikayo|ja|大友親世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Chikayo|1=Ōtomo Chikayo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433259}}, the {{ill|Governor of Bungo Province|ja|豊後国守護|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor of Bungo Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Chikayo donated shrine land and funds for construction. The shrine was established and named "Nagahama Daimyōjin"Katsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 1: Around Shōwa Avenue" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, August 17, 2007. The shrine was damaged by the tsunami caused by the {{ill|Keichō Bungo earthquake|ja|慶長豊後地震|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keichō Bungo earthquake|1=1596 Bungo earthquake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495196}} on {{ill|July 12 (Lunisolar)|lt=July 12|ja|7月12日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 12 (lunar calendar)}} in [[:en:Bunroku|Bunroku]] 5 (1596 CE). It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)|lt=Kasuga Shrine}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seie Town (Ōita)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|lt=Seie Town}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in [[:en:Yuanhe|Genna]] 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in [[:en:Enpō|Empō]] 9 (1681 CE). In [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō]] 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from [[:en:July 5|July 5]] to [[:en:July 7|July 7]], is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include [[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]] processions, [[:en:Kagura|Kagura]], and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to [[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the [[:en:Funai Domain|Fuchū domain (Bungo)]], [[:en:Matsudaira Chikayoshi|Matsudaira Chikayoshi]], was halted in 1922 but resumed in [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of [[:en:Ligustrum japonicum|Privet]] adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the [[:en:Enpō|Empō]] era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from [[:en:Ōita Station|Ōita Station]], [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|JR Kyushu]] == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} {{translated page|ja|長浜神社 (大分市)|version=102199076|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Gō-sha]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[ja:長浜神社]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Ōita Prefecture]] [[ja:長浜神社 (大分市)]] {{wikidata link|Q28682890}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

Devotees traditionally offer wooden plaques (ema) depicting male and female hina dolls when making prayersKatsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 12: Around Nagahama" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita012a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, November 2, 2007. == History == According to shrine tradition, Nagahama Shrine was founded in [[:en:Ōei|Ōei]] 13 (1406). A local resident of Nagahama had a divine dream, and the following morning discovered a small wooden shrine box washed ashore, containing a miniature altar inscribed "Yoshu Nagahama Daimyōjin" on the inner side of its door and a sacred mirror inside. Upon reporting this discovery to {{ill|Ōtomo Chikayo|ja|大友親世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Chikayo|1=Ōtomo Chikayo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433259}}, the {{ill|Governor of Bungo Province|ja|豊後国守護|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor of Bungo Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Chikayo donated shrine land and funds for construction. The shrine was established and named "Nagahama Daimyōjin"Katsumi Watanabe, "Digital Book Version of 'Ōita Past and Present' Chapter 1: Around Shōwa Avenue" {{PDF|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita001a1.pdf link]}} Ōita Gōdō Shimbun, August 17, 2007. The shrine was damaged by the tsunami caused by the {{ill|Keichō Bungo earthquake|ja|慶長豊後地震|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keichō Bungo earthquake|1=1596 Bungo earthquake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495196}} on {{ill|July 12 (Lunisolar)|lt=July 12|ja|7月12日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 12 (lunar calendar)}} in [[:en:Bunroku|Bunroku]] 5 (1596 CE). It was temporarily relocated to the grounds of {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)|ja|春日神社 (大分市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)|lt=Kasuga Shrine}} in {{ill|Seie Town (Ōita)|ja|勢家町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seie Town (Ōita)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|lt=Seie Town}} before moving to Shiokyūshō Town in [[:en:Yuanhe|Genna]] 5 (1619 CE), and again within the same town to its present location in [[:en:Enpō|Empō]] 9 (1681 CE). In [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō]] 2 (1913 CE), the neighboring Matsusue Tenmasha, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, was merged into Nagahama Shrine. == Summer Festival == Nagahama Shrine's annual summer festival, held from [[:en:July 5|July 5]] to [[:en:July 7|July 7]], is affectionately called "Nagahama-sama." It traces its origins to the "Nagahama Market," one of Bungo's three great markets, which began during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Because the festival often coincides with the rainy season, it is nicknamed "Rainy Nagahama-sama." It is recognized as Ōita's first summer festival of the year, signaling the start of summer[http://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/8708/ Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita Tourism Information Official Website[http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/events/detail/527 Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Ōita City Tourism Association[http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160707/ddl/k44/040/242000c Ōmikoshi Procession Highlighting Nagahama Shrine Festival in Ōita City] Mainichi Shimbun, July 7, 2016[https://www.oitabank.co.jp/hometown/kawara/kawara-article/150713.html Nagahama Shrine Summer Festival] Yacchoru Dē! Ōita Kawara-ban (Ōita Bank). The festival transforms Shiokyūshō Street and Nagahama Street into pedestrian-only zones, with over 120 stalls and displays of "Fūryū Mitate Saiku" (decorative floats). Highlights include [[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]] processions, [[:en:Kagura|Kagura]], and performances such as Yosakoi and hula dancing on the festival square's opening night. The mikoshi procession, dating back to [[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 3 (1856 CE) when it was first dedicated by the final feudal lord of the [[:en:Funai Domain|Fuchū domain (Bungo)]], [[:en:Matsudaira Chikayoshi|Matsudaira Chikayoshi]], was halted in 1922 but resumed in [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 62 (1987 CE). During the festival, an auspicious item called "Omika Mochi" is sold. Stems of [[:en:Ligustrum japonicum|Privet]] adorned with red, yellow, and green rice cakes and paper strips originated from a legend where a woman named Omika offered such decorations in gratitude for healing. The tradition dates to the [[:en:Enpō|Empō]] era and was revived postwar by the local preservation society. == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes on foot from [[:en:Ōita Station|Ōita Station]], [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|JR Kyushu]] == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * {{Facebook|nagahamajinjya.oita|Nagahama Shrine}} {{translated page|ja|長浜神社 (大分市)|version=102199076|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Gō-sha]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[ja:長浜神社]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Ōita Prefecture]] [[ja:長浜神社 (大分市)]] {{wikidata link|Q28682890}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]
